Scope And Evidence

  • Report scope: repo-matching Codex sessions in `/Users/prasannaarikala/.codex/sessions` plus authored git history reachable from the current branch `develop`.
  • Available session window: Mar 2, 2026 to Apr 20, 2026. No repo-matching archived Codex sessions were found in `~/.codex/archived_sessions`.
  • Author filter used for git activity: `Prasanna Arikala`. Commits with multiple local emails were included when they matched that author name.
  • Session end times are treated as last observed thread activity, not reliable active-effort duration, because threads can be reopened later.
  • Current uncommitted workspace state exists and is not counted in historical git metrics. The visible example in this worktree is `examples/retirement-service-demo/`.

Executive Readout

Across the locally available repo-matching Codex history, the work pattern starts with channel/runtime reviews in early March, moves into security and E2E hardening in mid-March, expands into UI, observability, NLU, and Helix analysis in late March and early April, and culminates in a dense April 18-20 delivery burst around Helix, ABL contract hardening, runtime behavior, and Studio serialization.

Git evidence shows sustained follow-through rather than just analysis: 3,228 authored commits across 50 active commit days, dominated by fixes, features, docs, tests, and refactors. The deepest product areas were apps/runtime, apps/studio, packages/helix, packages/compiler, and packages/project-io, which lines up with the session themes.

The recent burst from Apr 18, 2026 through Apr 20, 2026 is especially implementation-heavy: 180 commits landed across 3 days, centered on Helix cross-provider quorum convergence, ABL contract hardening, typed handoff/history authoring, import/apply/runtime safety, gather-editor loss prevention, and runtime handoff/reask/memory fixes.

1,219 fixes682 features519 docs commits282 test commits5,910 runtime touches4,603 studio touches1,288 helix touches

What The Work Was Actually About

  • Runtime and Studio platform work dominated the full window, especially execution flow, routing, session handling, serializer fidelity, editor safety, and API/UX hardening.
  • Helix became the biggest concentrated workstream in the recent window: executor scaffolding, dueling-plan orchestration, replay recovery, concerns scanning, doctor/readiness checks, and large test coverage additions.
  • ABL/compiler work was not isolated to docs. The evidence shows schema, parser, IR, contract-registry, and language-service changes landing together with runtime/studio follow-through and documentation sync.
  • Testing and documentation were first-class parts of delivery, not afterthoughts: the log shows heavy touches under docs/plans, docs/testing, docs/features, runtime and helix test suites, and post-implementation sync artifacts.
  • Session logs skew review-heavy because many threads were used to inspect, compare, or audit before code was written, but the commit stream shows those reviews translated into a high volume of concrete implementation work.

Review these issues and create plan to implement these with future ready design

 - G1 — POST /import/apply swallows the real error behind a generic IMPORT_APPLY_FAILED. Throw site: packages/project-io/src/import/core-direct-apply.ts:1073-1113. 
  Include stage + sanitized cause in the response.                                                                                                                   
  - G2 — locales/*.json at project root passes preview but crashes apply with G1's opaque error. Preview should reject with E_LOCALE_INVALID_PATH. Source:           
  packages/project-io/src/locale-files.ts.                                                                                                                           
  - G3 — Runtime ignores project.entryAgentName, falls back to project.agents[0].name. Fix: apps/runtime/src/routes/chat.ts:1759-1762.                               
  - G4 — Inline TOOLS: signatures don't auto-create Project Tools on apply; preview warns but apply is a no-op. Requires 39 manual PUTs.
  - G5 — Runtime preserves (arglist) suffix in tool lookup key. dsl_call fires check_outage_by_address(service_address) and registry has check_outage_by_address.    
  One-line fix at flow-step-executor.ts:1967 (+ siblings at :1600, :5775), or normalize in the compiler IR.
  - G11 — SET: x = result.field silently stores the literal string "result.user_id" when result is unset. Cascades from G5.                                          
                                                                                                                                                                     
  P1 — degrades UX                                     
                                                                                                                                                                     
  - G6 — compileABLtoIR() puts validator errors in compilation_errors, not errors. SDK users who only check errors miss semantic validation entirely.                
  - G12 — Template filters (| ago, etc.) fail open: raw {{user.field | ago}} reaches the user response.                                                              
                                                                                                                                                                     
  P2 — polish                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                     
  - G7 — Reserved-keyword asymmetry: THEN: complete and THEN: ESCALATE fail DANGLING_STEP_REF but THEN: COMPLETE works.                                              
  packages/compiler/src/platform/ir/validate-ir.ts:404.                                                                                                              
  - G8 — TIMEOUT: "30s" fails INVALID_TIMEOUT_SYNTAX. Only unquoted 30s works.
  - G9 — Preview previewDigest lives inside preview.previewDigest, not at response root. Undocumented.                                                               
  - G10 — /import/apply rate limit returns 429 with empty body, no Retry-After.
  - G13 — Duplicate constraint evaluation concatenates the same respond message twice.                                                                               
                                                                                                                     
  Observation (not a bug)                                                                                                                                            
                                                                                                                                                                     
  - G14 — LLM-driven tool calls work correctly (separate resolver strips signatures). Only the DSL CALL: path is broken by G5.

2026-04-14 22:45 IST 2026-04-14 22:47 IST Review / analysis Untitled thread 9
[Tue 2026-04-14 22:45 GMT+5:30] Run a closed-loop HELIX replay evaluation and tuning exercise in this repository. Goal:… 
[Tue 2026-04-14 22:45 GMT+5:30] Run a closed-loop HELIX replay evaluation and tuning exercise in this repository.

Goal:
Instead of asking whether HELIX "feels better," take a real change that was already implemented in this repo, rewind the codebase to the exact commit before that change, and ask the current HELIX to reproduce the same fix autonomously in a detached replay worktree. Then compare what HELIX produced against the known historical target commit.

For each scenario, measure:
- whether HELIX reaches the right root cause
- whether it touches the right files
- whether it avoids unnecessary scope drift
- whether it proves the change with the right tests
- whether it commits and exits cleanly
- how much time/turns it burns in each stage

Operating rule:
When HELIX stalls, over-explores, times out, widens scope, or fails to finalize correctly, do not patch the product feature manually. Patch HELIX itself or the replay harness, rebuild, rerun the exact same scenario, and observe whether the behavior improved. This is an iterative optimization loop over the harness, not over the product feature.

Execution guidance:
- Identify the best first historical scenario to use as the seed case and explain why.
- Run the replay in a detached replay worktree.
- Inspect replay runs under .helix/replays/runs/ and any relevant detached worktrees.
- Summarize progress clearly: replay state, fixes made, remaining blockers, and next action.
- If you change HELIX or the harness, build before tests: `pnpm --dir packages/helix build` before `pnpm --dir packages/helix test`.
- Compare resulting diffs against the historical target commit patch.
- Avoid unnecessary scope drift.
- Do not push unless explicitly asked.

Start now by selecting the seed scenario and beginning the first evaluation loop.

users are getting errors : when i am trying to use websdk after a certain while i am getting error that {"type":"error"… 
users are getting errors : when i am trying to use websdk after a certain while i am getting error that {"type":"error","message":"Concurrent session limit exceeded"}. 

 My workspace is enterprise but invited members are only able to land on the workspace once. they can't switch to my workspace after page reload or re-login.
this issue of concurrent session limit applies to pretty much all devs working on free plan.

invited users still have problems in using the apps in the workspace. Will get specific issues reported but increasing the limit to 10 and setting TTL for stale session to timeout and end is better.

  #: 1

    detectTableMetadata() for HTML format (untested — only CSV path tested). isAvailable() combined states. getStatus() conditional provider reporting.           
  Priority: HIGH                                                                                                                                                     
  ────────────────────────────────────────                                                                                                                        
  #: 2                                                                                                                                                               
  Area: Trace event mapping integrity                          
  Source File: packages/observatory/src/schema/trace-event-mappings.ts                                                                                               
  Testable Logic: Reverse mapping reduce has a last-writer-wins collision (delegate vs delegate_start → both map to agent.delegated). Need: completeness (all known
    categories present), no orphaned reverse entries, round-trip correctness for non-aliased types.                                                                  
  Priority: HIGH                                               
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  #: 3                                                                                                                                                               
  Area: Guardrail scoreToSeverity boundary
  Source File: packages/compiler/src/platform/guardrails/provider.ts                                                                                                 
  Testable Logic: Currently tested at mid-range values. Need: exact boundary values (0.2, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9), negative scores, scores >1.0, NaN — real edge cases the
    threshold comparison doesn't handle.
  Priority: MEDIUM
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  #: 4                                                                                                                                                               
  Area: Crawler DecisionEngine
  Source File: packages/crawler/src/decision/decision-engine.ts                                                                                                      
  Testable Logic: Construction removed. No real logic gap — 39 tests remain covering all 5 decision levels. Skip.
  Priority: NONE
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  #: 5                                                                                                                                                               
  Area: Session factory
  Source File: apps/runtime/src/services/session/session-factory.ts                                                                                                  
  Testable Logic: registerAgent, resumeSession, getSession are pure delegation — no branching, no transformation. The branching tests (channel→channelType, tool
    resolution) are kept. Skip.
  Priority: NONE
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  #: 6                                                                                                                                                               
  Area: MetricInfoIcon
  Source File: apps/studio/src/components/session/MetricInfoIcon.tsx                                                                                                 
  Testable Logic: Pure React UI component — dialog open/close, translation lookup. No domain logic. Skip.
  Priority: NONE
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  #: 7                                                                                                                                                               
  Area: a2a/ports, connectors/types, pipeline-engine/node-types, compiler/validation-types
  Source File: Various                                                                                                                                               
  Testable Logic: Pure type/interface definitions. Zero runtime logic. TypeScript compiler enforces correctness. Skip.
  Priority: NONE
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  #: 8                                                                                                                                                               
  Area: Search-AI middleware
  Source File: apps/search-ai/src/middleware/auth.ts                                                                                                                 
  Testable Logic: Removed 2 FIXME mock-echo tests + 1 export echo. The 15 remaining tests cover all real auth config logic. No gap.
  Priority: NONE
2026-04-09 20:28 IST 2026-04-18 15:34 IST Review / analysis Review Helix deep scan 2,082
waht do you think of helix's approach of deep scan and other code scan capabilties ? What do you think of this review:… 
waht do you think of helix's approach of deep scan and other code scan capabilties ? What do you think of this review:

 How Claude Code & Codex handle this                                                                                                                                
   
  Claude Code doesn't pre-scan at all. It uses on-demand tool calls — Glob to find files, Grep to search content, Read to look at specific files. The LLM decides    
  what to look at next based on the task. It never loads the full codebase into context. That's why it's fast — it's lazy evaluation, not eager scanning.
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Codex uses a similar tool-based approach but also builds a lightweight repo map at the start — a summary of the file tree + top-level symbols. Not full file       
  contents, just enough structure for the model to know where to look.
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Helix's deep scan is the opposite — it eagerly walks the entire scope, reads every file, and sends it all to the LLM in one shot. That's the fundamental           
  bottleneck.
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Open-source tools you could bundle

  ┌───────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┐    
  │         Tool          │                                 What it does                                 │   License   │            Bundling effort             │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ Aider's repo-map      │ tree-sitter AST → compact map of classes/functions/signatures per file. ~10x │ Apache 2.0  │ Medium — Python, but the algorithm is  │
  │                       │  smaller than raw source. The LLM sees structure, not code.                  │             │ portable to TS                         │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ tree-sitter           │ Language-agnostic incremental parser. Extracts AST from source in            │ MIT         │ Low — node-tree-sitter npm package     │
  │                       │ milliseconds. Powers repo-map, Cursor, Continue, Zed, Neovim.                │             │ exists, TS bindings ready              │    
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ SCIP (Sourcegraph)    │ Precise code intelligence — cross-repo symbol resolution, go-to-definition,  │ Apache 2.0  │ High — needs language-specific         │    
  │                       │ find-references. Much richer than regex-based export parsing.                │             │ indexers (scip-typescript exists)      │    
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Greptile              │ Code understanding API — you push a repo, it indexes, then you query.        │ Commercial  │ Low effort, but external dependency    │    
  │                       │ Handles embedding + retrieval.                                               │ API         │                                        │    
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ LanceDB               │ Embedded vector DB (no server). Store code chunk embeddings locally,         │ Apache 2.0  │ Low — npm package, runs in-process     │    
  │                       │ retrieve by similarity.                                                      │             │                                        │    
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Nomic Embed /         │ Local code embedding models. No API calls needed.                            │ Apache 2.0  │ Medium — needs ONNX runtime or similar │    
  │ jina-embeddings       │                                                                              │             │                                        │    
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Chromadb              │ Embedded vector store with built-in embedding functions                      │ Apache 2.0  │ Low — has a JS client                  │    
  └───────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────┘    
   
  What I'd actually recommend for Helix                                                                                                                              
                  
  The Aider repo-map approach is the most proven and directly applicable:                                                                                            
   
  1. tree-sitter parses each file → extracts function/class/type signatures (not bodies)                                                                             
  2. Build a repo map — a compact text representation (~10x smaller than source)
  3. Send repo map + feature spec to the deep scan model                                                                                                             
  4. The model identifies which files need full reading (maybe 10-20% of scope)                                                                                      
  5. Only those files get loaded in full                                                                                                                             
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Aider proved this works at scale — it handles repos with thousands of files and keeps context under 8K tokens for the map. The key insight is the LLM doesn't need 
  to read every line of code to understand what a file does — it needs signatures and structure.                                                                     
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Concrete integration path:

  npm install tree-sitter tree-sitter-typescript  # already have TS bindings
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Then in repo-index.ts, replace the regex-based parseExports/parseImportSpecifiers with tree-sitter AST queries, and add a buildRepoMap() function that produces a  
  compact summary instead of full file contents.                                                                                                                     
                                                                                                                                                                     
  The deep scan stage would then get:                                                                                                                                
  - Compact repo map (all files, signatures only) — small context
  - Feature spec                                                                                                                                                     
  - Full content of only the files the model requests

2026-03-22 14:32 IST 2026-03-22 14:37 IST Review / analysis Add gradient tokens discussion 14
Create a work-in-progress item in docs -- as a todo for this session and start working on the below and update What do… 
Create a work-in-progress item in docs -- as a todo for this session and start working on the below and update 

What do you think of this:

Prasanna: 

i want to include some gradients into thte design token system in some places to bring some character to the studio                                                                                          


Agent:

  Current gradient situation:
  - No --gradient-* CSS tokens exist — gradients are scattered as ad-hoc utility classes (.text-gradient, .arch-panel-bg, .sidebar-bg) and inline Tailwind (bg-gradient-to-r from-accent to-accent-muted)
  - Existing gradients are very subdued — mostly monochrome accent washes with low opacity

  Before I design the gradient token system, a few questions:

  1. Character / vibe — Are you thinking subtle brand-accented gradients (e.g., cool blue-to-purple shifts behind cards, headers), or bolder statement gradients (hero sections, CTA buttons, status badges
  with color pops)?
  2. Where specifically? Some candidates:
    - Page/section headers
    - Card borders or hover states
    - Primary CTA buttons
    - Sidebar / panel backgrounds
    - Status indicators (success/error/warning with gradient dimension)
    - The topology canvas background
    - Empty states / onboarding
    - Something else?
  3. Color palette direction — The current palette is very monochrome (grays + accent). Should gradients introduce new hues (blue→purple, teal→emerald) or stay within the existing semantic colors?
  4. Light theme too? — Should gradients adapt for both dark and light themes, or is this primarily a dark-theme enhancement?


Prasanna: 

❯ THis is a tool for agent developers but also enterprise focused -- i do want to bring some character into this -- what do you suggest?                                                                       
                                                                                                                                         
Agent:

                                         
  Design Direction: "Ambient Intelligence"                                                                                                                                                                     
   
  The current palette is clean but flat. For an AI agent platform, gradients should feel like ambient light and energy — suggesting intelligence, activity, and depth without screaming "startup landing page."
   Enterprise users trust restraint; developers appreciate polish.
                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  The Gradient Language                                                                                                                                                                                        
   
  1. Brand / AI Signature — Purple → Blue (your --purple and --info already exist)                                                                                                                             
  - This becomes the platform's visual identity for "AI is here"
  - Used on: primary CTAs, Arch AI panel, agent creation, active/live indicators                                                                                                                               
  - Subtle but recognizable — when a user sees this gradient, they know it means "AI-powered"
                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  2. Ambient Depth — Monochrome shifts with a hint of color                                                                                                                                                    
  - Barely perceptible background washes that add dimensionality                                                                                                                                               
  - Used on: page backgrounds, card surfaces, sidebar                                                                                                                                                          
  - Think: a dark room with a faint colored light source in one corner                                                                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  3. Status Energy — Semantic color pairs that feel alive                                                                                                                                                      
  - Success: green → teal (growth, completion)                                                                                                                                                                 
  - Warning: amber → orange (heat, attention)                                                                                                                                                                  
  - Error: red → rose (urgency without alarm)                                                                                                                                                                  
  - These replace flat status backgrounds on key moments — deploy success, pipeline completion, alert banners                                                                                                  
                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  4. Accent Borders — Gradient borders on interactive elements                                                                                                                                                 
  - Agent cards on hover get a subtle gradient border (purple→blue)                                                                                                                                            
  - Selected/active states use gradient borders instead of solid                                                                                                                                               
  - This is the highest-character, lowest-risk move — it adds energy to interactions without changing the resting state                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  Where to Deploy (High Impact, Enterprise-Safe)                                                                                                                                                               
                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┐                                                                                           
  │                  Location                  │    Gradient Type     │                    Why                     │                                                                                           
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤                                                                                           
  │ Primary CTA buttons (Deploy, Create Agent) │ Brand purple→blue    │ Draws the eye to key actions               │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Arch AI panel header/accent                │ Brand purple→blue    │ Reinforces "AI assistant"                  │                                                                                           
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤                                                                                           
  │ Agent card hover borders                   │ Accent border        │ Adds life to the main workspace            │                                                                                           
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤                                                                                           
  │ Topology canvas ambient                    │ Radial ambient       │ Makes the canvas feel like a living system │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤                                                                                           
  │ Empty states                               │ Radial ambient       │ Turns dead space into inviting space       │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤                                                                                           
  │ Success/deploy banners                     │ Status energy        │ Makes achievements feel rewarding          │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤                                                                                           
  │ Page section headers                       │ Subtle text gradient │ Adds polish to headings                    │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤                                                                                           
  │ Sidebar active indicator                   │ Brand accent         │ Replaces flat highlight with dimension     │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────┘                                                                                           
                                                            
  What I'd Avoid                                                                                                                                                                                               
                                                            
  - Gradients on body text or data tables (readability)                                                                                                                                                        
  - Loud gradients on resting card states (visual noise at scale)
  - Rainbow/multicolor anything (kills enterprise trust)                                                                                                                                                       
                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  Implementation Plan                                                                                                                                                                                          
                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  I'd add --gradient-* CSS custom properties to the token system (with dark/light variants), map them to Tailwind utilities, and then apply them to the spots above. The tokens would reference existing       
  semantic colors so everything stays coherent.

❯ there are inconsistencies and flaky experience with observatory UI -- on selecting events, the parent child window el… 
❯ there are inconsistencies and flaky experience with observatory UI -- on selecting events, the parent child window element relationships ; there are some      
  inconstencies and redundancies; reivew thoroughly and explain
2026-03-21 09:29 IST 2026-03-21 09:34 IST Review / analysis Audit reusable agent plan 55
Review this thourhgly to identify the gaps, existing implemantion tat already supports this feature,and come up with an… 
Review this thourhgly to identify the gaps, existing implemantion tat already supports this feature,and come up with an implmenation plan that is thorough and has e2e sceanrios and test cases covered


[FloridaBlue] Reusable Agent Modules — Build once, import everywhere across projects



Description

Problem
Customers building multiple agent applications (projects) on the platform find themselves duplicating common functionality across projects. For example, an identity verification flow, a payment processing agent, or a FAQ handler may be needed in several different applications. Today, the only option is to copy-paste the agent definitions and tools into each project individually.

This leads to:

Duplication: The same agent logic exists in multiple projects, each copy diverging over time

Maintenance burden: A bug fix or improvement to a shared capability must be applied to every project that uses it

Inconsistency: Different projects end up with slightly different versions of the same capability, causing inconsistent user experiences

Slower development: Building a new application means re-implementing common patterns instead of composing from proven building blocks

Requirement
As a customer building multiple agent applications on the platform,
I want to build a reusable agent module in one place and import it into any of my projects,
so that common capabilities are maintained once and stay consistent across all applications that use them.

Think of it like a Python module or an npm package — you define it once, publish it, and import it wherever you need it.

What "Reusable" Means
A reusable module should be a self-contained unit of agent functionality that can be used across projects. It could contain:

One or more agents — e.g., an "Identity Verification" module with a verification agent and a fraud-check sub-agent

Tools — custom tools that the module's agents use (API integrations, data lookups, etc.)

Gather flows — structured data collection sequences (e.g., collecting shipping address, verifying insurance details)

Configuration — default settings that the importing project can override (e.g., which identity documents are accepted, retry limits)

A module is not a full application on its own — it doesn't have its own entry point or its own sessions besides the development test sessions. It's a building block that becomes part of the importing project when used. However, a module should have its own deployment lifecycle with environment tags (e.g., dev, qa, prod), so that the module team can develop and test improvements without affecting projects that are already using a released version. An importing project pins to a specific environment/version of the module — a module being actively worked on in dev does not impact a project running the prod tag.

Expected Behaviors
Create a module: A user should be able to designate an entire project as a reusable module. The module has a name, version, and description. It defines what it provides (agents, tools) and what it requires from the importing project (configuration, credentials, data).

Import a module into a project: A project should be able to declare a dependency on a module. The module's agents and tools become available within the importing project — they can be referenced in handoffs, delegations, and routing rules as if they were defined locally.

Version and environment management: Modules should support environment-based promotion (dev → qa → prod), just like application projects. The module team works on improvements in dev, tests in qa, and promotes to prod when ready. An importing project pins to an environment tag or a specific version — a project running in production pulls from the module's prod tag, so in-progress development on the module never affects live applications. Updating to a new version is an explicit action, not automatic.

Configuration and customization: The importing project should be able to configure the module's behavior without modifying the module itself. For example:

Override default settings (e.g., change the greeting message, adjust thresholds)

Provide project-specific credentials (e.g., the module uses a payment API — each project provides its own API key)

Map the module's expected data fields to the project's own data model

Isolation: A module running within a project should respect the importing project's tenant isolation, data retention policies, and security boundaries. The module does not introduce its own data scope — it operates within the importing project's context.

Discoverability: Users should be able to browse available modules within their tenant. Optionally, a tenant admin could publish modules that are available to all projects within the tenant.

Testing: A module should be testable in isolation (e.g., via a test harness or sandbox project) before being published for use by other projects.

User Journey Example
A health insurance provider builds multiple agent applications on the platform to serve different audiences:

Member Services Bot — helps members with general inquiries, ID cards, claims status, and complaints

Provider Portal Agent — assists referring physicians and facilities with eligibility checks, prior authorizations, and claim dispute resolution

Enrollment Assistant — guides prospective and renewing members through plan selection, enrollment, and onboarding

HR Benefits Portal Agent — supports employer-group HR teams managing their employees' benefits packages

All four applications need a benefits queries agent — an agent that can look up plan details, explain coverage levels, check deductibles and out-of-pocket maximums, compare plans, and answer "is this procedure covered?" questions. The underlying benefits logic is the same regardless of who's asking: a member checking their own coverage, a physician verifying a patient's eligibility, a prospective member comparing plans, or an HR administrator reviewing what's available to their employees.

Today, the provider's team has copy-pasted the benefits queries agent into all four projects. Each copy has its own version of the plan lookup tools, formulary search, and coverage explanation logic. When the organization introduced a new plan tier mid-year, the team updated the benefits agent in Member Services and Provider Portal but missed Enrollment and HR Portal — those apps showed stale plan information for weeks before anyone noticed, leading to member confusion and support escalations.

With reusable modules, the team creates:

benefits-queries@1.0 — contains the benefits query agent, plan lookup tools, formulary search, and coverage explanation logic

Each of the four projects imports this module. The benefits agent is maintained once by the core team. When the new plan tier is added, benefits-queries@1.1 is published and each project team upgrades on their own schedule. The Member Services project configures it to use plain-language explanations for members, while the Provider Portal configures it to return clinical and billing detail for physicians — same module, different configuration.

Constraints and Considerations
Scope boundaries: What happens when a module's agent needs data that lives in the importing project's context (e.g., customer account info)? The contract between module and project needs to be clear — what the module expects as input and what it provides as output.

Credential isolation: Modules may need API credentials (e.g., a payment gateway key). These must come from the importing project, not be baked into the module. A module should never carry secrets.

Conflict resolution: What if two imported modules define agents or tools with the same name? The platform needs a namespacing or conflict resolution strategy.

Permissions: Who can create modules? Who can publish them tenant-wide? Who can import them? This ties into the platform's existing role and permission model.

Update propagation: When a module is updated, importing projects should not be affected until they explicitly upgrade. But there should be visibility into which projects use which module version (dependency graph).

Debugging and observability: When a module's agent is executing within a project, traces and logs should clearly indicate that the agent comes from a module — making it easy to attribute issues to the module vs. the project.

Tenant scoping: Modules are scoped to a single tenant. Cross-tenant agent interaction is out of scope for modules and should be handled via external protocols like A2A.
2026-03-21 09:29 IST 2026-03-21 09:33 IST Review / analysis Audit reusable agent test plan 53

Testing: A module should be testable in isolation (e.g., via a test harness or sandbox project) before being published for use by other projects.

Enrollment Assistant — guides prospective and renewing members through plan selection, enrollment, and onboarding

Description

Problem

Duplication: The same agent logic exists in multiple projects, each copy diverging over time

Maintenance burden: A bug fix or improvement to a shared capability must be applied to every project that uses it

Inconsistency: Different projects end up with slightly different versions of the same capability, causing inconsistent user experiences

Slower development: Building a new application means re-implementing common patterns instead of composing from proven building blocks

Requirement
As a customer building multiple agent applications on the platform,
I want to build a reusable agent module in one place and import it into any of my projects,
so that common capabilities are maintained once and stay consistent across all applications that use them.

Updating to a new version is an explicit action, not automatic.

Configuration and customization: The importing project should be able to configure the module's behavior without modifying the module itself. For example:

Override default settings (e.g., change the greeting message, adjust thresholds)

Provide project-specific credentials (e.g., the module uses a payment API — each project provides its own API key)

Map the module's expected data fields to the project's own data model

Isolation: A module running within a project should respect the importing project's tenant isolation, data retention policies, and security boundaries. The module does not introduce its own data scope — it operates within the importing project's context.

Discoverability: Users should be able to browse available modules within their tenant. Optionally, a tenant admin could publish modules that are available to all projects within the tenant.

Testing: A module should be testable in isolation (e.g., via a test harness or sandbox project) before being published for use by other projects.

User Journey Example
A health insurance provider builds multiple agent applications on the platform to serve different audiences:

Member Services Bot — helps members with general inquiries, ID cards, claims status, and complaints

Provider Portal Agent — assists referring physicians and facilities with eligibility checks, prior authorizations, and claim dispute resolution

Enrollment Assistant — guides prospective and renewing members through plan selection, enrollment, and onboarding

HR Benefits Portal Agent — supports employer-group HR teams managing their employees' benefits packages

All four applications need a benefits queries agent — an agent that can look up plan details, explain coverage levels, check deductibles and out-of-pocket maximums, compare plans, and answer "is this procedure covered?" questions. The underlying benefits logic is the same regardless of who's asking: a member checking their own coverage, a physician verifying a patient's eligibility, a prospective member comparing plans, or an HR administrator reviewing what's available to their employees.

Today, the provider's team has copy-pasted the benefits queries agent into all four projects. Each copy has its own version of the plan lookup tools, formulary search, and coverage explanation logic. When the organization introduced a new plan tier mid-year, the team updated the benefits agent in Member Services and Provider Portal but missed Enrollment and HR Portal — those apps showed stale plan information for weeks before anyone noticed, leading to member confusion and support escalations.

With reusable modules, the team creates:

benefits-queries@1.0 — contains the benefits query agent, plan lookup tools, formulary search, and coverage explanation logic

Each of the four projects imports this module. The benefits agent is maintained once by the core team. When the new plan tier is added, benefits-queries@1.1 is published and each project team upgrades on their own schedule. The Member Services project configures it to use plain-language explanations for members, while the Provider Portal configures it to return clinical and billing detail for physicians — same module, different configuration.

Constraints and Considerations
Scope boundaries: What happens when a module's agent needs data that lives in the importing project's context (e.g., customer account info)? The contract between module and project needs to be clear — what the module expects as input and what it provides as output.

Credential isolation: Modules may need API credentials (e.g., a payment gateway key). These must come from the importing project, not be baked into the module. A module should never carry secrets.

Conflict resolution: What if two imported modules define agents or tools with the same name? The platform needs a namespacing or conflict resolution strategy.

Permissions: Who can create modules? Who can publish them tenant-wide? Who can import them? This ties into the platform's existing role and permission model.

Update propagation: When a module is updated, importing projects should not be affected until they explicitly upgrade. But there should be visibility into which projects use which module version (dependency graph).

Debugging and observability: When a module's agent is executing within a project, traces and logs should clearly indicate that the agent comes from a module — making it easy to attribute issues to the module vs. the project.

Tenant scoping: Modules are scoped to a single tenant. Cross-tenant agent interaction is out of scope for modules and should be handled via external protocols like A2A.
2026-03-21 09:29 IST 2026-03-21 09:32 IST Review / analysis Review reusable agent modules plan 50
Review this thourhgly to identify the gaps, existing implemantion tat already supports this feature,and come up with an… 
Review this thourhgly to identify the gaps, existing implemantion tat already supports this feature,and come up with an implmenation plan that is thorough and has e2e sceanrios and test cases covered


[FloridaBlue] Reusable Agent Modules — Build once, import everywhere across projects



Description

Problem
Customers building multiple agent applications (projects) on the platform find themselves duplicating common functionality across projects. For example, an identity verification flow, a payment processing agent, or a FAQ handler may be needed in several different applications. Today, the only option is to copy-paste the agent definitions and tools into each project individually.

This leads to:

Duplication: The same agent logic exists in multiple projects, each copy diverging over time

Maintenance burden: A bug fix or improvement to a shared capability must be applied to every project that uses it

Inconsistency: Different projects end up with slightly different versions of the same capability, causing inconsistent user experiences

Slower development: Building a new application means re-implementing common patterns instead of composing from proven building blocks

Requirement
As a customer building multiple agent applications on the platform,
I want to build a reusable agent module in one place and import it into any of my projects,
so that common capabilities are maintained once and stay consistent across all applications that use them.

Think of it like a Python module or an npm package — you define it once, publish it, and import it wherever you need it.

What "Reusable" Means
A reusable module should be a self-contained unit of agent functionality that can be used across projects. It could contain:

One or more agents — e.g., an "Identity Verification" module with a verification agent and a fraud-check sub-agent

Tools — custom tools that the module's agents use (API integrations, data lookups, etc.)

Gather flows — structured data collection sequences (e.g., collecting shipping address, verifying insurance details)

Configuration — default settings that the importing project can override (e.g., which identity documents are accepted, retry limits)

A module is not a full application on its own — it doesn't have its own entry point or its own sessions besides the development test sessions. It's a building block that becomes part of the importing project when used. However, a module should have its own deployment lifecycle with environment tags (e.g., dev, qa, prod), so that the module team can develop and test improvements without affecting projects that are already using a released version. An importing project pins to a specific environment/version of the module — a module being actively worked on in dev does not impact a project running the prod tag.

Expected Behaviors
Create a module: A user should be able to designate an entire project as a reusable module. The module has a name, version, and description. It defines what it provides (agents, tools) and what it requires from the importing project (configuration, credentials, data).

Import a module into a project: A project should be able to declare a dependency on a module. The module's agents and tools become available within the importing project — they can be referenced in handoffs, delegations, and routing rules as if they were defined locally.

Version and environment management: Modules should support environment-based promotion (dev → qa → prod), just like application projects. The module team works on improvements in dev, tests in qa, and promotes to prod when ready. An importing project pins to an environment tag or a specific version — a project running in production pulls from the module's prod tag, so in-progress development on the module never affects live applications. Updating to a new version is an explicit action, not automatic.

Configuration and customization: The importing project should be able to configure the module's behavior without modifying the module itself. For example:

Override default settings (e.g., change the greeting message, adjust thresholds)

Provide project-specific credentials (e.g., the module uses a payment API — each project provides its own API key)

Map the module's expected data fields to the project's own data model

Isolation: A module running within a project should respect the importing project's tenant isolation, data retention policies, and security boundaries. The module does not introduce its own data scope — it operates within the importing project's context.

Discoverability: Users should be able to browse available modules within their tenant. Optionally, a tenant admin could publish modules that are available to all projects within the tenant.

Testing: A module should be testable in isolation (e.g., via a test harness or sandbox project) before being published for use by other projects.

User Journey Example
A health insurance provider builds multiple agent applications on the platform to serve different audiences:

Member Services Bot — helps members with general inquiries, ID cards, claims status, and complaints

Provider Portal Agent — assists referring physicians and facilities with eligibility checks, prior authorizations, and claim dispute resolution

Enrollment Assistant — guides prospective and renewing members through plan selection, enrollment, and onboarding

HR Benefits Portal Agent — supports employer-group HR teams managing their employees' benefits packages

All four applications need a benefits queries agent — an agent that can look up plan details, explain coverage levels, check deductibles and out-of-pocket maximums, compare plans, and answer "is this procedure covered?" questions. The underlying benefits logic is the same regardless of who's asking: a member checking their own coverage, a physician verifying a patient's eligibility, a prospective member comparing plans, or an HR administrator reviewing what's available to their employees.

Today, the provider's team has copy-pasted the benefits queries agent into all four projects. Each copy has its own version of the plan lookup tools, formulary search, and coverage explanation logic. When the organization introduced a new plan tier mid-year, the team updated the benefits agent in Member Services and Provider Portal but missed Enrollment and HR Portal — those apps showed stale plan information for weeks before anyone noticed, leading to member confusion and support escalations.

With reusable modules, the team creates:

benefits-queries@1.0 — contains the benefits query agent, plan lookup tools, formulary search, and coverage explanation logic

Each of the four projects imports this module. The benefits agent is maintained once by the core team. When the new plan tier is added, benefits-queries@1.1 is published and each project team upgrades on their own schedule. The Member Services project configures it to use plain-language explanations for members, while the Provider Portal configures it to return clinical and billing detail for physicians — same module, different configuration.

Constraints and Considerations
Scope boundaries: What happens when a module's agent needs data that lives in the importing project's context (e.g., customer account info)? The contract between module and project needs to be clear — what the module expects as input and what it provides as output.

Credential isolation: Modules may need API credentials (e.g., a payment gateway key). These must come from the importing project, not be baked into the module. A module should never carry secrets.

Conflict resolution: What if two imported modules define agents or tools with the same name? The platform needs a namespacing or conflict resolution strategy.

Permissions: Who can create modules? Who can publish them tenant-wide? Who can import them? This ties into the platform's existing role and permission model.

Update propagation: When a module is updated, importing projects should not be affected until they explicitly upgrade. But there should be visibility into which projects use which module version (dependency graph).

Debugging and observability: When a module's agent is executing within a project, traces and logs should clearly indicate that the agent comes from a module — making it easy to attribute issues to the module vs. the project.

Tenant scoping: Modules are scoped to a single tenant. Cross-tenant agent interaction is out of scope for modules and should be handled via external protocols like A2A.
2026-03-21 00:54 IST 2026-03-21 11:05 IST Review / analysis Plan reusable agent modules feature 103
Review this thourhgly to identify the gaps, existing implemantion tat already supports this feature,and come up with an… 
Review this thourhgly to identify the gaps, existing implemantion tat already supports this feature,and come up with an implmenation plan that is thorough and has e2e sceanrios and test cases covered


[FloridaBlue] Reusable Agent Modules — Build once, import everywhere across projects



Description

Problem
Customers building multiple agent applications (projects) on the platform find themselves duplicating common functionality across projects. For example, an identity verification flow, a payment processing agent, or a FAQ handler may be needed in several different applications. Today, the only option is to copy-paste the agent definitions and tools into each project individually.

This leads to:

Duplication: The same agent logic exists in multiple projects, each copy diverging over time

Maintenance burden: A bug fix or improvement to a shared capability must be applied to every project that uses it

Inconsistency: Different projects end up with slightly different versions of the same capability, causing inconsistent user experiences

Slower development: Building a new application means re-implementing common patterns instead of composing from proven building blocks

Requirement
As a customer building multiple agent applications on the platform,
I want to build a reusable agent module in one place and import it into any of my projects,
so that common capabilities are maintained once and stay consistent across all applications that use them.

Think of it like a Python module or an npm package — you define it once, publish it, and import it wherever you need it.

What "Reusable" Means
A reusable module should be a self-contained unit of agent functionality that can be used across projects. It could contain:

One or more agents — e.g., an "Identity Verification" module with a verification agent and a fraud-check sub-agent

Tools — custom tools that the module's agents use (API integrations, data lookups, etc.)

Gather flows — structured data collection sequences (e.g., collecting shipping address, verifying insurance details)

Configuration — default settings that the importing project can override (e.g., which identity documents are accepted, retry limits)

A module is not a full application on its own — it doesn't have its own entry point or its own sessions besides the development test sessions. It's a building block that becomes part of the importing project when used. However, a module should have its own deployment lifecycle with environment tags (e.g., dev, qa, prod), so that the module team can develop and test improvements without affecting projects that are already using a released version. An importing project pins to a specific environment/version of the module — a module being actively worked on in dev does not impact a project running the prod tag.

Expected Behaviors
Create a module: A user should be able to designate an entire project as a reusable module. The module has a name, version, and description. It defines what it provides (agents, tools) and what it requires from the importing project (configuration, credentials, data).

Import a module into a project: A project should be able to declare a dependency on a module. The module's agents and tools become available within the importing project — they can be referenced in handoffs, delegations, and routing rules as if they were defined locally.

Version and environment management: Modules should support environment-based promotion (dev → qa → prod), just like application projects. The module team works on improvements in dev, tests in qa, and promotes to prod when ready. An importing project pins to an environment tag or a specific version — a project running in production pulls from the module's prod tag, so in-progress development on the module never affects live applications. Updating to a new version is an explicit action, not automatic.

Configuration and customization: The importing project should be able to configure the module's behavior without modifying the module itself. For example:

Override default settings (e.g., change the greeting message, adjust thresholds)

Provide project-specific credentials (e.g., the module uses a payment API — each project provides its own API key)

Map the module's expected data fields to the project's own data model

Isolation: A module running within a project should respect the importing project's tenant isolation, data retention policies, and security boundaries. The module does not introduce its own data scope — it operates within the importing project's context.

Discoverability: Users should be able to browse available modules within their tenant. Optionally, a tenant admin could publish modules that are available to all projects within the tenant.

Testing: A module should be testable in isolation (e.g., via a test harness or sandbox project) before being published for use by other projects.

User Journey Example
A health insurance provider builds multiple agent applications on the platform to serve different audiences:

Member Services Bot — helps members with general inquiries, ID cards, claims status, and complaints

Provider Portal Agent — assists referring physicians and facilities with eligibility checks, prior authorizations, and claim dispute resolution

Enrollment Assistant — guides prospective and renewing members through plan selection, enrollment, and onboarding

HR Benefits Portal Agent — supports employer-group HR teams managing their employees' benefits packages

All four applications need a benefits queries agent — an agent that can look up plan details, explain coverage levels, check deductibles and out-of-pocket maximums, compare plans, and answer "is this procedure covered?" questions. The underlying benefits logic is the same regardless of who's asking: a member checking their own coverage, a physician verifying a patient's eligibility, a prospective member comparing plans, or an HR administrator reviewing what's available to their employees.

Today, the provider's team has copy-pasted the benefits queries agent into all four projects. Each copy has its own version of the plan lookup tools, formulary search, and coverage explanation logic. When the organization introduced a new plan tier mid-year, the team updated the benefits agent in Member Services and Provider Portal but missed Enrollment and HR Portal — those apps showed stale plan information for weeks before anyone noticed, leading to member confusion and support escalations.

With reusable modules, the team creates:

benefits-queries@1.0 — contains the benefits query agent, plan lookup tools, formulary search, and coverage explanation logic

Each of the four projects imports this module. The benefits agent is maintained once by the core team. When the new plan tier is added, benefits-queries@1.1 is published and each project team upgrades on their own schedule. The Member Services project configures it to use plain-language explanations for members, while the Provider Portal configures it to return clinical and billing detail for physicians — same module, different configuration.

Constraints and Considerations
Scope boundaries: What happens when a module's agent needs data that lives in the importing project's context (e.g., customer account info)? The contract between module and project needs to be clear — what the module expects as input and what it provides as output.

Credential isolation: Modules may need API credentials (e.g., a payment gateway key). These must come from the importing project, not be baked into the module. A module should never carry secrets.

Conflict resolution: What if two imported modules define agents or tools with the same name? The platform needs a namespacing or conflict resolution strategy.

Permissions: Who can create modules? Who can publish them tenant-wide? Who can import them? This ties into the platform's existing role and permission model.

Update propagation: When a module is updated, importing projects should not be affected until they explicitly upgrade. But there should be visibility into which projects use which module version (dependency graph).

Debugging and observability: When a module's agent is executing within a project, traces and logs should clearly indicate that the agent comes from a module — making it easy to attribute issues to the module vs. the project.

Tenant scoping: Modules are scoped to a single tenant. Cross-tenant agent interaction is out of scope for modules and should be handled via external protocols like A2A.

[FloridaBlue] Omnichannel Session Continuity — Cross-Channel Recall and Live Transcript Sync



Description

Problem
End users interact with our agents across multiple channels — web chat, mobile chat, voice calls. Today, each channel interaction is an isolated session. When a user starts on web chat and later calls in by phone, the agent has no awareness of the previous conversation. The user has to repeat themselves, leading to a poor experience.

Additionally, there is no way for a user who is on a voice call to simultaneously view or interact with the conversation via a text-based channel.

Requirements
This feature has two parts. Part 1 is the foundation; Part 2 builds on top of it.

Part 1: Cross-Channel Conversation Recall
As a user who has previously chatted with the agent on one channel (e.g., web chat or mobile chat),
when I later contact the agent on a different channel (e.g., voice call),
I want the agent to recall information from my previous session when I ask about it,
so that I don't have to repeat myself and the experience feels continuous.

Preconditions:

The user must be identified. The platform must know it's the same person across channels. This can happen through:

The agent gathering identity information during the conversation (e.g., phone number, email, account number)

The embedding application providing a consistent user identifier across channels

Channel-native identification (e.g., caller ID matching a previously stored phone number)

The agent project must have a programmable way to trigger identity linking — i.e., the agent builder can define when and how to associate a session with a known contact. This is not automatic for anonymous users.

Expected behaviors:

When the agent gathers identity information (phone, email, account number, etc.) during a session, the platform should store this and use it for future cross-channel matching

When a user starts a new session on a different channel, the platform should attempt to resolve them against known contacts using available identity signals

When a known contact starts a new session, the agent should have access to relevant context from previous sessions — not just structured data, but the ability to recall what was actually discussed.

When the user asks something like "what did we discuss last time?" or "you told me to do X," the agent should be able to retrieve and reference the previous conversation

The amount of previous context available to the agent should be bounded and configurable — for example:

How many previous sessions to consider

How far back in time

How many messages or tokens of context to load

The agent builder should have control over whether cross-session recall is enabled and how it behaves

The agent project should have a function to explicitly link sessions and also explicitly merge.

Out of scope for Part 1:

Automatic identity recognition for fully anonymous users (no gathered identity signals)

Real-time multi-channel interaction (that's Part 2)

Part 2: Live Omnichannel Transcript Sync
As a user who is currently on a voice call with the agent,
when I open the web chat widget at the same time,
I want to see the live transcript of the voice conversation in the chat window and be able to respond by either speaking or typing,
so that I have flexibility in how I interact and can reference the conversation visually.

Preconditions:

The user must be identified on both channels (the platform knows it's the same person on the call and in the chat)

A voice session must be actively in progress

Expected behaviors:

When an identified user opens the web chat while on an active voice call, the chat should detect the ongoing session and offer to show the live transcript (rather than starting a new, separate session)

The voice conversation transcript should appear in the chat window in real time — both the user's spoken words (transcribed) and the agent's responses

If the user joins the chat mid-call, they should see the conversation history up to that point (backfill)

The user should be able to type a response in the chat window instead of speaking — the agent treats it as part of the same conversation regardless of input channel

The agent's response should be delivered to both channels — spoken via voice and displayed as text in the chat.

Each message should indicate which channel it came from (voice vs. typed) so the user has clear visual context

If the voice call ends, the chat session should be able to continue as a standalone text conversation

If the chat window is closed, the voice call should continue unaffected

User Journey Example
Sarah is a customer of Acme Insurance. She opens the web chat on her phone and asks the agent about her claim status. The agent looks up her claim (claim #4821) and tells her the adjuster needs photos of the damage. Sarah says she'll upload them later and ends the chat.

Two days later, Sarah calls Acme's support line. The voice agent recognizes her (caller ID matches the phone number she provided during the web chat). Sarah says: "I uploaded the photos you asked for — what's the status now?"

The agent recalls the previous web chat, knows she's referring to claim #4821 and the photo request, and checks the current status without asking Sarah to re-explain.

While on the call, Sarah opens the web chat to see the transcript. She can see the agent's spoken responses as text. When the agent reads out a long reference number, Sarah can see it in the chat instead of writing it down. She types a follow-up question in the chat while still on the call, and the agent responds to both channels.

Constraints and Considerations
Privacy: Cross-session recall involves retaining and surfacing conversation content. The feature should respect data retention policies and any applicable consent requirements.

Performance: Loading previous session context should not noticeably delay session startup. Consider lazy loading (recall on demand) vs. eager loading (pre-load at session start).

Cost: Injecting previous conversation context into the LLM consumes tokens. Configurable limits are essential.

Channel diversity: The solution should work across all supported channels (web, mobile, voice, SMS, WhatsApp, etc.), not just web-to-voice.

Agent builder control: Not every agent project needs recall or live sync. These should be opt-in capabilities that the builder configures.
