2026-03-20 11:23 IST 2026-03-20 11:29 IST Review / analysis Assess plan for retain ABL 11
Review and tell me what do you think of this : Yes, partially. The **code changes so far are mostly compatible** with “… 
Review and tell me what do you think of this :

Yes, partially. The **code changes so far are mostly compatible** with “retain all ABL constructs,” but the **plan is internally inconsistent** and still contains removal-oriented instructions that conflict with that requirement.

**Findings**
- **High:** The plan still tells us to delete phase syntax from examples. It explicitly says “Remove phase headers, flatten to `REQUIRE`/`WARN` lists,” which conflicts with retaining ABL constructs. See [2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md](/Users/prasannaarikala/projects/agent-platform/docs/plans/2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md#L860) and the file matrix entries that repeat “remove phase headers/phases” at [2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md](/Users/prasannaarikala/projects/agent-platform/docs/plans/2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md#L1123).
- **High:** The plan still frames `BEFORE`, `IMPLIES`, `LIMIT`, and `RESTRICT` as “add to spec or remove from examples/docs.” Under your rule, the “remove” branch is invalid. See [2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md](/Users/prasannaarikala/projects/agent-platform/docs/plans/2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md#L55), [2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md](/Users/prasannaarikala/projects/agent-platform/docs/plans/2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md#L131), and [2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md](/Users/prasannaarikala/projects/agent-platform/docs/plans/2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md#L959).
- **Medium:** The plan’s Phase 2 is still too aggressive for a retain-all-constructs policy. It aims to remove the legacy evaluator, but the audits show remaining gaps in guardrail re-entry, reasoning-path `ON_FAIL`, and handoff semantics. That means fallback removal is not yet safe if we want to preserve behavior across all ABL surfaces. See [2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md](/Users/prasannaarikala/projects/agent-platform/docs/plans/2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md#L38), [2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md](/Users/prasannaarikala/projects/agent-platform/docs/plans/2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md#L655), and [2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md](/Users/prasannaarikala/projects/agent-platform/docs/plans/2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md#L1814).
- **Medium:** The audit doc still recommends “remove phase syntax from the DSL,” which now conflicts with both your requirement and the newer compiler warning approach. See [dsl-feedback-review-2026-03-18.md](/Users/prasannaarikala/projects/agent-platform/docs/audit/dsl-feedback-review-2026-03-18.md#L376).

**What Is Not Broken**
- The actual implementation we landed does **not** remove ABL syntax. The compiler still accepts the constructs it accepted before, and the new work is additive/safety-oriented: `ON_FAIL` parsing, keyword handling, `W822`, `W823`, and targeted field clearing in [compiler.ts](/Users/prasannaarikala/projects/agent-platform/packages/compiler/src/platform/ir/compiler.ts#L334).

**Bottom Line**
The plan is **not unusable**, but it **is broken as written for your stated requirement**. It needs to be rewritten from:

- “implement or remove”

to:

- “retain every ABL surface construct”
- “implement semantics where ready”
- “emit warnings/notes where semantics are deferred”
- “never flatten/delete syntax from examples or docs just because runtime support is incomplete”

**Recommended Rewrite**
- Keep phase syntax and document it as label-only for now; do not flatten examples.
- Keep `BEFORE`, `IMPLIES`, `LIMIT`, and `RESTRICT` in examples/docs; mark them as retained-but-deferred until semantics are implemented.
- Split Phase 2 so legacy fallback removal happens only after parity for guardrails, reasoning `ON_FAIL`, handoff, and current-turn `input` context.

If you want, I can make the next pass purely on the docs/plan and turn it into a strict **retain-all-ABL-constructs** plan.


---

Yes. If the rule is **“retain all ABL constructs”**, then the plan should be rewritten around **retention + semantics + warnings**, not **implement-or-remove**.

The clean way to think about it is:

- `Supported`: parser + compiler + runtime + docs all agree.
- `Retained with warning`: syntax stays, compiler accepts it, runtime semantics are partial or label-only, docs say so plainly.
- `Retained with lowering`: syntax stays, compiler rewrites it to simpler IR/CEL.
- `Retained but deferred`: syntax stays, compiler warns, examples/docs can show it, but we do not pretend it works fully yet.

Here’s the brainstorming pass item by item.

**Phase Names**
- The plan is broken here because it still says to flatten/remove phase headers in examples. That conflicts with retention. See [2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md](/Users/prasannaarikala/projects/agent-platform/docs/plans/2026-03-18-dsl-constraints-guardrails-cleanup-plan.md#L860).
- Option A: keep phase names as **labels only** forever. Preserve `phase.name` in AST/IR/trace output, warn on non-`always`, but do not give them execution semantics.
- Option B: make phase names real runtime gates. That means inventing a mapping from names like `pre_booking` to steps, intents, or state transitions.
- Option C: keep labels only, and if users want real gating, introduce a different explicit construct later such as `WHEN` or `APPLIES_WHEN`.
- My lean: **Option C**. Phase names are too ambiguous to be reliable execution semantics, but they are still useful authoring/grouping syntax.

**`BEFORE`**
- This is the hardest construct because current examples use it in more than one way.
- One interpretation is temporal/checkpoint: `REQUIRE x BEFORE calling search_aggregate`.
- Another interpretation is almost implication-like: `REQUIRE icloud_backup_confirmed == true BEFORE reset_type == "factory"`.
- Option A: define `BEFORE` as a true checkpoint/precondition operator only, and only support structural RHS targets like `calling tool`, `step`, `field`, `action`.
- Option B: overload it. Structural RHS means checkpoint; boolean RHS means implication.
- Option C: retain it, warn on every use, and postpone semantics.
- My lean: **Option A with a compatibility shim**. Long-term, `BEFORE` should mean checkpoint/precondition only. For old boolean-shaped uses, the compiler can temporarily warn and suggest `IMPLIES`.

**`IMPLIES`**
- This one is worth supporting rather than punting.
- Option A: parser/compiler lowers `A IMPLIES B` to `!(A) || (B)`.
- Option B: keep it accepted but warning-only until later.
- Option C: support only simple top-level `IMPLIES` first, and require parentheses for more complex cases.
- My lean: **A + C together**. Implement it, but be strict about precedence so we don’t create subtle bugs.

**`LIMIT`**
- This needs a product meaning, not just a parser decision.
- Option A: make `LIMIT` a pure alias of `REQUIRE`.
- Option B: retain it as distinct IR metadata, but runtime initially treats it the same as `REQUIRE`.
- Option C: give it distinct semantics now, like clamp/reask/correct rather than hard block.
- My lean: **Option B**. Keep the syntax, compile it to `constraintKind: "limit"`, and let runtime behavior initially alias existing hard-constraint behavior until you decide the UX.

**`RESTRICT`**
- Same shape as `LIMIT`, but with a different likely intent.
- Option A: alias to `REQUIRE`.
- Option B: preserve as distinct kind in IR, but same runtime behavior initially.
- Option C: define it as stronger policy semantics now, like deny/escalate/hide.
- My lean: **Option B** again. Retain the author intent in IR now; specialize later.

**`WHEN` on constraints**
- This one is actually a good candidate for explicit support.
- Option A: keep it as a compiler-level modifier and lower it to implication semantics.
- Option B: keep it only as parser syntax and warn.
- My lean: **Option A**. It is a clearer way to express gating than phase names.

**Legacy Evaluator Removal**
- This is where the current plan is most dangerous.
- If all constructs must be retained, then fallback removal can only happen after every retained construct has either:
- a CEL lowering,
- a structured IR form plus runtime handler,
- or a compile-time hard block that you explicitly approve.
- Option A: remove fallback now. I would not do this.
- Option B: keep fallback until parity is proven across all retained constructs and expression surfaces.
- Option C: keep fallback only for retained-but-not-yet-lowered constructs.
- My lean: **Option B first**, then maybe C later. Right now the audits still show runtime gaps.

**Constraint `HANDOFF`**
- This is not just a docs issue; it affects whether a retained construct is truthful.
- Option A: implement real handoff behavior for constraint failures.
- Option B: keep parsing it, but compile-warn that runtime does not execute a true handoff yet.
- Option C: compile-error it until implemented.
- My lean: if you want to retain it publicly, **Option A** is best. If you want to be conservative for one cycle, **Option B** is acceptable.

**Reasoning-path `ON_FAIL` semantics**
- Right now flow and reasoning do not fully agree.
- Option A: unify all checkpoints on the same violation handler semantics.
- Option B: leave reasoning more limited and document it.
- My lean: **Option A**. If the construct is retained, semantics should not depend on which executor path happened to run.

**Guardrail re-entry**
- This is not an ABL syntax retention question, but it affects the plan.
- Option A: pipeline is the only guardrail path; `checkConstraints` handles constraints only.
- Option B: keep both modern and legacy paths for now.
- My lean: **Option A**. It reduces duplication and makes retained guardrail syntax more predictable.

**What I’d change in the plan**
- Replace every “remove from examples/docs” branch with “retain, mark status honestly.”
- Replace “remove phase headers” with “preserve phase labels; warn that they are label-only.”
- Reframe Phase 2 from “delete legacy evaluator” to “earn deletion by parity.”
- Add a construct matrix: `construct`, `retained?`, `current semantics`, `target semantics`, `compiler behavior`, `runtime owner`, `docs status`.

My default decision set would be:

1. Phase names: retain as labels only.
2. `BEFORE`: retain; checkpoint semantics only; warn on ambiguous boolean RHS.
3. `IMPLIES`: implement.
4. `WHEN`: implement as compiler-level gating.
5. `LIMIT` / `RESTRICT`: retain as distinct IR kinds, runtime alias for now.
6. `HANDOFF` in constraints: implement or warn loudly.
7. Legacy fallback: do not remove yet.

If you want, I can turn this into a **replacement decision table** directly inside the plan doc so you can mark each item as approved/rejected.


----


If we keep all constructs, the practical question for each one is: **what value does it add beyond what we already have, and what is the closest working substitute today?**

**Phase Names**
- Example: `CONSTRAINTS: pre_booking: - REQUIRE guest_email IS SET`
- Value: grouping, readability, traceability, easier review. It tells a human “these rules belong together.”
- Existing working alternative today: plain declaration order plus comments. The current runtime already behaves as if phase names are labels only, so this construct already has value even without gating.
- My take: phase names are worth retaining even if they stay non-executable labels.

**`BEFORE`**
- Example: `REQUIRE measure_field IS SET BEFORE calling search_aggregate`
- Example: `REQUIRE icloud_backup_confirmed == true BEFORE reset_type == "factory"`
- Value: expresses prerequisite relationships in business language instead of low-level boolean rewrites.
- Existing working alternative today for tool/step preconditions: explicit `CHECK` or an earlier scripted step before the `CALL`.
- Existing working alternative today for boolean-shaped cases: rewrite as implication logic, for example `REQUIRE reset_type != "factory" OR icloud_backup_confirmed == true`.
- Existing working alternative today for field dependency: `activation: progressive` with `depends_on`, or just collect/order the fields differently.
- My take: this construct has real value, but it is overloaded right now. The likely decision is whether `BEFORE` should mean only structural checkpoints, or also allow boolean-style shorthand.

**`IMPLIES`**
- Example: `REQUIRE dispute_type == "card" IMPLIES card_unique_id != ""`
- Value: much more readable than manual implication rewrites for conditional rules.
- Existing working alternative today: `REQUIRE dispute_type != "card" OR card_unique_id != ""`
- My take: this one has high value and low conceptual risk. It is the easiest “retain and implement” candidate.

**`LIMIT` as a constraint keyword**
- Example: `LIMIT guests <= 10`
- Value: expresses “this is a cap/threshold” instead of a generic business rule.
- Existing working alternative today for validation thresholds: `REQUIRE guests <= 10 ON_FAIL: RESPOND "Max 10 guests"`
- Existing working alternative today for soft behavior: `WARN guests <= 10`
- Existing working alternative today for collection truncation: there is already a separate working `LIMIT:` in transform/list contexts, so part of the decision is whether constraint-level `LIMIT` should mean something different.
- My take: keep it, but decide whether it is just author-intent metadata at first or a truly different runtime behavior.

**`RESTRICT`**
- Example: `RESTRICT destination IN blocked_countries`
- Value: expresses policy prohibition more clearly than a generic `REQUIRE`.
- Existing working alternative today for business rules: `REQUIRE NOT (destination IN blocked_countries) ON_FAIL: BLOCK "Unsupported destination"`
- Existing working alternative today for safety/content policy: GUARDRAILS already cover that better than CONSTRAINTS.
- My take: it has value if you want a distinct policy category. If not, it becomes a stylistic alias of `REQUIRE`.

**Constraint-level `WHEN` / applicability gating**
- Example: `WHEN channel == "voice": REQUIRE ssn IS NOT SET`
- Value: cleaner than stuffing applicability into every condition.
- Existing working alternative today: fold the applicability into the condition, for example `REQUIRE channel != "voice" OR ssn IS NOT SET`
- Existing working alternative in other parts of the language: `when` already exists in several ABL areas, so this would fit the language style.
- My take: this has good value because it solves the “phase names should not be execution semantics” problem cleanly.

**`ON_FAIL: HANDOFF`**
- Example: `REQUIRE refund_amount <= 1000 ON_FAIL: HANDOFF refund_specialist "High-value refund"`
- Value: escalation becomes part of business rules instead of being pushed into separate flow logic.
- Existing working alternative today: set a flag / respond / escalate in the constraint path, then use normal routing or supervisor handoff rules to move to another agent.
- My take: this construct is valuable, but only if it performs a real handoff. If not, it should be retained but clearly marked as partial.

**Phase labels vs real gating**
- Example: `pre_payment:` and `pre_booking:` blocks
- Value: labels are low-friction organization; real gating is stronger but much more ambiguous.
- Existing working alternative today for real gating: encode the gate directly in the condition, for example `REQUIRE current_stage != "payment" OR card_token IS SET`
- My take: the construct has value even as a label-only feature. The real question is whether you want a separate explicit gating construct instead of overloading labels.

**Where the existing system already gives you good substitutes**
- For conditional business rules, `REQUIRE ... OR ...` already works.
- For field prerequisites, `activation`, `depends_on`, and gather ordering already work.
- For tool/step preconditions, scripted `CHECK`/step structure already works.
- For safety restrictions, GUARDRAILS are a better existing solution than inventing too much behavior in CONSTRAINTS.
- For escalation/routing, supervisor handoff rules are a better existing solution than fake constraint handoff semantics.

**My shortest read**
- Strongest value, easiest to justify: `IMPLIES`, `WHEN`, phase labels.
- Valuable but needs semantic design: `BEFORE`.
- Valuable only if you want distinct policy vocabulary: `LIMIT`, `RESTRICT`.
- Valuable only if fully real: `ON_FAIL: HANDOFF`.

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision sheet** with one recommendation per construct: `retain as label`, `retain and implement`, `retain but alias existing behavior`, or `retain but warn`.
2026-03-20 07:23 IST 2026-03-20 08:41 IST Review / analysis Review multi tenancy security gaps 60
Review these - are they valid concerns 1. any authenticated user on the platform can read/modify/delete any other user'… 
Review these - are they valid concerns

1.   any authenticated user on the platform can read/modify/delete any other user's projects across tenant boundaries, since there's no ownership or tenant check. -- src/app/api/projects/[id]/route.ts:24-49 — GET, PATCH, and DELETE all call requireAuth but never verify the authenticated user owns or has tenant access to the project.

2.  Debug Token in plaintext - src/app/api/debug/token/route.ts:20-30 — The debug token is stored unhashed in the database, then looked up via findUnique({ where: { token } }) in debug/validate. Contrast with refresh tokens which are properly SHA-256 hashed before storage. A database leak would expose all debug tokens.

3.  WebSocket Auth Token in URL Query String
src/contexts/WebSocketContext.tsx:387 — The JWT access token is sent as a URL query parameter. Tokens in URLs get logged in server access logs, proxy logs, browser history, and Referer headers. The standard pattern is to send the token in the first WS message after connection, or use a short-lived ticket.

4. Rate Limiter Unbounded Memory Growth
src/lib/rate-limit.ts — The in-memory Map never evicts expired entries. Over time, the map grows without bound as new IPs hit the server. There's no periodic cleanup or size cap. This is a slow memory leak that will eventually cause OOM in long-running deployments.

5.  No Role-Based Authorization on Sensitive Operations
The JWT includes a role field (OWNER, ADMIN, OPERATOR, MEMBER, VIEWER), but requireAuth() in lib/auth.ts only verifies the user exists — it never checks the role. Sensitive endpoints like creating SDK keys, managing credentials, or deleting projects have no role enforcement. A VIEWER can do everything an OWNER can.

6. Credential Routes Missing Tenant Scoping
src/app/api/credentials/route.ts:27 — Credentials are scoped to userId only:
where: { userId: user.id }
But SDK keys correctly scope to project + tenant. If credentials are meant to be per-workspace (shared among team members), this is wrong. If per-user, then the POST route should also verify the user's tenant membership before allowing creation.

2026-03-06 00:06 IST 2026-03-06 00:10 IST Review / analysis Research unifying delegate handoff 12
Whats your view on this ? how do we now think of unifying delegate and handoff ? research and lets brainstorm ⏺ Skill(b… 
Whats your view on this ?


 how do we now think of unifying delegate and handoff ? research and lets brainstorm 

⏺ Skill(brainstorming)
  ⎿  Successfully loaded skill

⏺ Using the brainstorming skill to explore unifying delegate and handoff. Let me start by understanding the current state of both
  constructs.

⏺ 4 agents finished (ctrl+o to expand)
   ├─ Explore (Research delegate implementation) · 40 tool uses · 73.0k tokens
   │  ⎿  Done
   ├─ Explore (Research handoff implementation) · 41 tool uses · 78.1k tokens
   │  ⎿  Done
   ├─ Explore (Research fan-out implementation) · 27 tool uses · 46.3k tokens
   │  ⎿  Done
   └─ Agent (Research competitor approaches) · 19 tool uses · 29.7k tokens
      ⎿  Done

⏺ Good research complete. Here's what I've found across the codebase and industry:

  Current State: Three Constructs

  ┌──────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┐
  │                  │            Handoff             │             Delegate              │             Fan-Out             │
  ├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Semantics        │ Transfer conversation to agent │ Call agent as subtask, get result │ Parallel subtasks               │
  ├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ User interaction │ User talks to child            │ No user interaction               │ No user interaction             │
  ├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Parent           │ Paused or completed            │ Blocked until done                │ Blocked until all done          │
  ├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Thread           │ Persistent (waiting/active)    │ Ephemeral                         │ Ephemeral (pruned)              │
  ├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Result flow      │ Data merge on return           │ USE_RESULT + RETURNS mapping      │ _last_fan_out + per-target vars │
  ├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Return path      │ __return_to_parent__ (new)     │ Automatic on completion           │ Automatic on all complete       │
  └──────────────────┴────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘

  Industry Landscape

  - OpenAI Agents SDK: Two constructs — handoffs (transfer) vs agent.as_tool() (subtask)
  - Google ADK: Two constructs — transfer_to_agent vs AgentTool
  - LangGraph: Two patterns via graph topology (handoff tools vs supervisor-calls-workers)
  - CrewAI: Unified delegation-only model (task-oriented, no transfer concept)
  - AutoGen: Message-passing primitive, Swarm layer adds handoff semantics

  3 of 5 platforms explicitly separate "transfer" from "call as subtask." The split is driven by a real semantic difference: does the
  user talk to the child agent?

  Now, here's my first clarifying question:

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · What's the primary motivation for unification — is it simplifying the DSL surface area for agent authors, or simplifying
     the runtime engine internals, or both? → Customer mental model

⏺ Got it — the driver is the customer mental model. Customers see all agents as interactive entities, and having a separate "delegate"
   construct that makes agents non-interactive is confusing. Let me dig into that.

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · Currently, DELEGATE means 'call agent, no user interaction, get result back.' If we unify under HANDOFF, how should the
     'no user interaction' subtask pattern work? → Drop subtask pattern entirely

⏺ Bold choice. So the principle is: agents are always interactive, tools are for function calls. This means if you need a
  non-interactive computation, you write a TOOL, not an agent.

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · Fan-out currently dispatches to both agents AND tools in parallel. Under the 'agents are interactive' model, fan-out to
     agents doesn't make sense (can't have parallel interactive conversations). What happens to fan-out? → Fan-out auto-routes
     results

⏺ Interesting — so fan-out becomes sequential dispatching where each child agent gets a turn interactively. Let me clarify what
  "interactively" means in this context.

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · When fan-out dispatches to agents sequentially, does each child agent get to talk to the user (multi-turn), or does it get
      one shot at the user's message and must return? → One-shot per child

⏺ OK so I now have a clear picture of the vision. Let me synthesize the approaches.

  Summary of the Unified Model

  Core principle: Agents are always interactive. Non-interactive computation = use a TOOL.

  This means:
  - DELEGATE is removed. The __delegate__ system tool, coordination.delegates IR field, and DSL section go away.
  - HANDOFF is the only agent-to-agent construct. It always transfers the conversation. RETURN:true means the child can return to
  parent (already implemented). RETURN:false means permanent transfer.
  - Fan-out becomes one-shot sequential dispatch. Each child agent gets the user message, processes it in one turn (with tool access),
   returns result. Parent synthesizes.

  Now here are 3 approaches for how to execute this unification:

  ---
  Approach A: Big Bang — Remove DELEGATE, Unify Everything Now (Recommended)

  What changes:
  1. Remove DELEGATE: DSL section from parser
  2. Remove coordination.delegates from IR schema
  3. Remove __delegate__ tool injection from prompt-builder
  4. Remove handleDelegate() from routing-executor
  5. Migrate existing DELEGATE use cases to either HANDOFF (if interactive) or TOOL (if computation)
  6. Refactor fan-out to be sequential one-shot, remove parallel agent threads

  Pros: Clean cut, no legacy baggage, no confusion
  Cons: Breaking change for any existing DSL files using DELEGATE. Requires migration path.

  ---
  Approach B: Deprecate-then-Remove — Keep DELEGATE as Sugar

  What changes:
  1. DELEGATE in DSL compiles to a HANDOFF with RETURN:true + internal _one_shot:true flag
  2. Runtime treats _one_shot handoffs as "send one message, auto-complete"
  3. __delegate__ tool calls get internally translated to __handoff__ calls
  4. After migration period, remove DELEGATE DSL section entirely

  Pros: No breaking changes, gradual migration, backward compatible
  Cons: Two code paths for a while, complexity during transition

  ---
  Approach C: Runtime-Only Unification — Keep Both DSL Constructs, Merge Engine

  What changes:
  1. DSL keeps both HANDOFF and DELEGATE sections (familiar to users)
  2. Both compile to the same IR structure (coordination.handoffs with flags)
  3. Runtime has one handleAgentTransfer() method that handles all cases
  4. Behavior controlled by flags: interactive, return, one_shot

  Pros: No DSL breaking changes, simpler engine, progressive disclosure
  Cons: Still two constructs in DSL, doesn't fully address the mental model issue

  ---

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · Which approach aligns with your vision for the unified agent model? → are there considerations to keep delegate?

⏺ Great question. Let me think through the cases for keeping DELEGATE.

  Cases For Keeping Delegate

  1. Performance / Latency

  DELEGATE runs in one synchronous call — no thread resume overhead, no __return_to_parent__ tool injection, no threadStack
  management. For a quick "calculate the price" call, handoff overhead is unnecessary. However, this is an implementation detail, not
  a semantic one — a one-shot HANDOFF could be equally fast with a flag.

  2. No Conversation Pollution

  DELEGATE threads are ephemeral and pruned. The child's internal reasoning never enters the parent's conversation history. With
  HANDOFF, the child's response gets pushed into the parent's history as [AgentName]: response. For a "look up inventory" subtask, you
   don't want that in the user's conversation. However, this is solvable — a SILENT:true or VISIBLE:false flag on HANDOFF could
  suppress conversation injection.

  3. Structured Input/Output Contract

  DELEGATE has INPUT: {field: source} and RETURNS: {result_key: target_key} — a function-call contract. HANDOFF has CONTEXT: pass:
  [fields] — a context-passing contract. The DELEGATE model is tighter and more predictable. However, HANDOFF with RETURN already has
  ON_RETURN.MAP which does the same structured mapping.

  4. Timeout & Failure Semantics

  DELEGATE has ON_FAILURE: continue | escalate | respond — the parent can gracefully handle failure. HANDOFF doesn't have this because
   the child is interactive and failure is a conversation-level concept. If we merge, we'd need to add failure handling to HANDOFF,
  which muddies the interactive semantics.

  5. Cycle/Depth Safety

  DELEGATE has delegateStack with depth limits (MAX_DELEGATE_DEPTH=10). HANDOFF has handoffStack with cycle detection. Different
  safety models because DELEGATE can recurse (A delegates to B which delegates to C) while HANDOFF is usually flat. If we merge, we
  need both safety models in one path.

  6. Remote Agent Protocol (A2A)

  Both DELEGATE and HANDOFF support LOCATION: remote. But DELEGATE's request/response pattern maps cleanly to A2A's tasks/send →
  tasks/get pattern. HANDOFF's interactive pattern is harder to map to A2A (requires streaming or long-polling). Removing DELEGATE
  could make remote agent integration harder.

  ---
  The Counter-Arguments

  Every point above is addressable with flags on HANDOFF:
  - Performance → ONE_SHOT: true (skip thread resume overhead)
  - Conversation pollution → SILENT: true (suppress conversation injection)
  - Structured I/O → Already have ON_RETURN.MAP
  - Failure handling → Add ON_FAILURE to HANDOFF
  - Depth safety → Unified stack with both cycle and depth checks
  - Remote → A2A protocol adapter handles both patterns

  The real question is: Do the flags make HANDOFF more confusing than having two constructs? If HANDOFF gets ONE_SHOT, SILENT,
  ON_FAILURE, INPUT, RETURNS, TIMEOUT — it starts looking like DELEGATE reborn under a different name.

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · Given these considerations, where do you land? The trade-off is: two constructs (clear semantics, more to learn) vs one
     construct with flags (unified model, more complex HANDOFF). → Rename + reframe

⏺ Smart choice. So the path is: keep both interaction modes (they're semantically real) but rename DELEGATE to something that clicks
  and unify the runtime engine where possible.

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · What name resonates best for the 'call agent as subtask' pattern? The mental model is: HANDOFF = 'transfer the user to
     this agent' vs [NEW_NAME] = 'ask this agent to do something and bring back the answer'. → None of them work

⏺ Fair enough. Let me think about this differently. The problem might be that all these names still frame it as "doing something to an
   agent" rather than expressing the relationship naturally.

  What if we approach naming from the customer's mental model — how would a non-technical person describe these two patterns?

  - "Transfer the customer to billing" → HANDOFF (transfer)
  - "Get a price check from the calculator" → ???

  Some other angles:

  ┌────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┐
  │    Name    │             Framing              │           Example            │              Feel               │
  ├────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ USE        │ "Use this agent for a task"      │ USE: Price_Calculator        │ Simple but vague                │
  ├────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ SUBTASK    │ Explicit about what it is        │ SUBTASK: Price_Calculator    │ Descriptive but long            │
  ├────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ REQUEST    │ "Make a request to this agent"   │ REQUEST: Price_Calculator    │ Natural but ambiguous with HTTP │
  ├────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ DISPATCH   │ "Dispatch work to this agent"    │ DISPATCH: Price_Calculator   │ Operational, clear direction    │
  ├────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ ASSIST     │ "Get assistance from this agent" │ ASSIST: Price_Calculator     │ Collaborative feel              │
  ├────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ TASK       │ "Give this agent a task"         │ TASK: Price_Calculator       │ Clean, aligns with CrewAI       │
  ├────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ RUN        │ "Run this agent"                 │ RUN: Price_Calculator        │ Short, imperative               │
  ├────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ AGENT_TOOL │ Google ADK's name                │ AGENT_TOOL: Price_Calculator │ Explicit but compound           │
  └────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · Do any of these resonate, or do you have a different name in mind? What's the word that would make a Kore.ai customer
     immediately understand 'this agent works for me behind the scenes'? → what do OpenAI uses this as?

⏺ OpenAI Agents SDK calls it agent.as_tool() — literally "use this agent as a tool."

  The API looks like:

  # Transfer pattern (handoff)
  triage_agent = Agent(
      name="Triage",
      handoffs=[billing_agent, support_agent],  # can transfer to these
  )

  # Subtask pattern (agent-as-tool)
  manager_agent = Agent(
      name="Manager",
      tools=[billing_agent.as_tool(
          tool_name="consult_billing",
          tool_description="Get billing info for the customer"
      )],
  )

  The key insight: they don't have a separate DSL keyword. An agent-as-tool is just an agent added to the tools list. The distinction
  is in where you put it — handoffs vs tools.

  Google ADK is similar — AgentTool(agent) wraps an agent into a tool.

  This suggests a radical simplification for ABL: Instead of DELEGATE as a separate section, what if agents can simply be listed as
  tools?

  AGENT: Booking_Agent

  TOOLS:
    - search_hotels(...)
    - Price_Calculator    # This IS an agent, but used as a tool
    - check_inventory(...)

  HANDOFF:
    - TO: Payment_Agent
      RETURN: true

  The agent author doesn't think "delegate vs handoff" — they think "this is a tool I use" vs "this is an agent I send the user to."

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · Does the 'agent-as-tool' approach resonate? Agents listed in TOOLS: behave like current DELEGATE (one-shot, result back).
     Agents listed in HANDOFF: transfer the conversation. → No, keep separate section

⏺ OK so you want: keep a dedicated section (visually distinct for agent-to-agent coordination) but rename it to something that clicks.
   And none of the names I proposed worked.

  Let me take a different approach — what is the mental model you're going for? Let me think about this from Kore.ai's existing
  platform language.

  In Kore.ai's current (legacy) platform, the concepts are:
  - Dialog Tasks — multi-turn conversations (like our HANDOFF)
  - Sub-intents — lightweight tasks within a dialog
  - Service Nodes — call external services (closest to DELEGATE)
  - Agent Transfer — transfer to human or another bot

  Kore.ai customers are used to thinking in terms of tasks and transfers.

  What if we lean into Kore.ai's own vocabulary?

  ┌───────────┬───────────┬───────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Current  │ Proposed  │         Customer reads as         │
  ├───────────┼───────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ HANDOFF:  │ TRANSFER: │ "Transfer the user to this agent" │
  ├───────────┼───────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ DELEGATE: │ SUBAGENT: │ "Use this as a sub-agent"         │
  └───────────┴───────────┴───────────────────────────────────┘

  Or staying closer to the industry:
  - HANDOFF stays HANDOFF (well understood)
  - DELEGATE becomes... let me just ask directly.

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · What word would YOU use to describe 'ask another agent to do a task and bring back the result'? Sometimes the best name
     comes from how you naturally describe it. → What about execute? or Invoke?

⏺ Both are strong. Let me compare them:

  ┌──────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                  │                              EXECUTE:                               │               INVOKE:                │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Reads as         │ "Execute this agent"                                                │ "Invoke this agent"                  │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Connotation      │ Run a command, get result. Imperative.                              │ Call upon an expert, get answer.     │
  │                  │                                                                     │ Formal.                              │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Industry         │ Shell/CLI feel. "Execute a script."                                 │ API/service feel. "Invoke a          │
  │ parallel         │                                                                     │ function."                           │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ ABL context      │ execution: already exists in IR (execution mode). Potential naming  │ No existing collision in ABL         │
  │                  │ collision.                                                          │ vocabulary.                          │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Customer feel    │ Slightly technical/mechanical                                       │ Professional, agent-like             │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ With example     │ EXECUTE: Price_Calculator                                           │ INVOKE: Price_Calculator             │
  └──────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────┘

  My recommendation: INVOKE — it avoids collision with the existing execution: IR concept, has a cleaner connotation ("invoke an
  expert" rather than "execute a program"), and maps well to how Google ADK and OpenAI frame it (invoke an agent, get a result).

  EXECUTE risks confusion because ABL already has execution.mode: reasoning | scripted in the IR, and the
  RuntimeExecutor.executeMessage() method. Too many "execute" concepts.

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · Between EXECUTE and INVOKE, which do you prefer? My recommendation is INVOKE to avoid collision with the existing
     'execution' concept in ABL IR. → INVOKE (Recommended)

⏺ INVOKE it is. Now let me put together the full design. Let me first consolidate what we've decided and present the design sections.

⏺ Design: Unified Agent Orchestration — HANDOFF + INVOKE

  Section 1: Mental Model & Positioning

  Two ways to work with agents:

  ┌───────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   │                        HANDOFF:                        │       INVOKE: (renamed from DELEGATE)        │
  ├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Customer reads as │ "Transfer the user to this agent"                      │ "Invoke this agent to get a result"          │
  ├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ User interaction  │ User talks to the child agent                          │ No user interaction — one-shot               │
  ├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Parent behavior   │ Pauses (RETURN:true) or exits (RETURN:false)           │ Blocks until result comes back               │
  ├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Result flow       │ Child responds directly to user; data merges on return │ Result returned to parent as structured data │
  ├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Analogy           │ "Let me transfer you to billing"                       │ "Let me check with our pricing team"         │
  └───────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  The key reframe: Both are agent-to-agent coordination. HANDOFF is "send the user there." INVOKE is "ask them for me." The customer
  doesn't need to think about threads, sessions, or interactivity — just "who talks to the user."

  Fan-out evolution: Fan-out dispatches become one-shot sequential — each child agent gets the user's message, processes it in one
  turn, returns result. Parent synthesizes. This aligns with INVOKE semantics (agents as experts you consult).

  Does Section 1 look right so far?

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · Does this mental model framing work? HANDOFF = 'send the user there', INVOKE = 'ask them for me'. → Looks good, continue

⏺ Section 2: DSL Syntax Changes

  INVOKE replaces DELEGATE in DSL:

  # Before (DELEGATE)
  DELEGATE:
    - AGENT: Price_Calculator
      WHEN: needs_pricing == true
      PURPOSE: "Calculate total price with taxes"
      INPUT: { hotel_id: selected_hotel_id, dates: booking_dates }
      RETURNS: { total: total_price, taxes: tax_amount }
      USE_RESULT: price_breakdown
      TIMEOUT: 15s
      ON_FAILURE: continue

  # After (INVOKE) — same fields, just renamed section
  INVOKE:
    - AGENT: Price_Calculator
      WHEN: needs_pricing == true
      PURPOSE: "Calculate total price with taxes"
      INPUT: { hotel_id: selected_hotel_id, dates: booking_dates }
      RETURNS: { total: total_price, taxes: tax_amount }
      USE_RESULT: price_breakdown
      TIMEOUT: 15s
      ON_FAILURE: continue

  HANDOFF stays the same — no syntax changes. The RETURN:true + __return_to_parent__ system from our recent implementation continues
  to work.

  Fan-out syntax stays the same in DSL — the runtime behavior changes (sequential one-shot instead of parallel ephemeral) but the
  __fan_out__ tool schema doesn't need DSL-level changes.

  Backward compatibility: Parser accepts both DELEGATE: and INVOKE: during a transition period. DELEGATE: emits a deprecation warning.
   Both compile to the same IR field (renamed from coordination.delegates to coordination.invocations).

  Section 3: IR Schema Changes

  // Before
  interface CoordinationConfig {
    delegates: DelegateConfig[];
    handoffs: HandoffConfig[];
    escalation?: EscalationConfig;
  }

  // After
  interface CoordinationConfig {
    invocations: InvocationConfig[];  // renamed from delegates
    handoffs: HandoffConfig[];
    escalation?: EscalationConfig;
  }

  // InvocationConfig is DelegateConfig renamed — same fields
  interface InvocationConfig {
    agent: string;
    when: string;
    purpose: string;
    input: Record<string, string>;
    returns: Record<string, string>;
    use_result: string;
    timeout?: string;
    on_failure: 'continue' | 'escalate' | 'respond';
    failure_message?: string;
    remote?: RemoteAgentLocation;
  }

  Section 4: Runtime System Tool Rename

  __delegate__  →  __invoke__

  - Constant: SYSTEM_TOOL_DELEGATE → SYSTEM_TOOL_INVOKE
  - Tool injection in prompt-builder reads from coordination.invocations
  - handleDelegate() → handleInvoke() in routing-executor
  - delegateStack → invocationStack in session
  - Trace events: delegate_start → invoke_start, delegate_complete → invoke_complete

  Section 5: Fan-Out Changes

  Current fan-out runs agent tasks in parallel ephemeral threads. Under the unified model:

  1. Agent tasks become sequential one-shot: Each agent gets the user message, runs one reasoning turn (may call tools), returns
  result
  2. Tool tasks remain parallel: HTTP tools can still run concurrently
  3. Implementation: Agent tasks use handleInvoke() internally instead of creating parallel child sessions
  4. Result synthesis: Parent LLM still receives all results and synthesizes a unified response

  This simplifies the fan-out engine significantly — no more child sessions, semaphores, or parallel thread management for agent
  tasks.

  Section 6: Migration Path

  1. Parser accepts both DELEGATE: and INVOKE: — DELEGATE: emits deprecation warning
  2. IR accepts both delegates and invocations — delegates mapped to invocations at load time
  3. Runtime handles both __delegate__ and __invoke__ tool calls — __delegate__ mapped internally
  4. After one release cycle, remove DELEGATE: parser support and delegates IR field

  Section 7: What Doesn't Change

  - HANDOFF syntax and behavior (unchanged)
  - __return_to_parent__ tool (just implemented)
  - Thread resume logic (just implemented)
  - ESCALATE (unchanged)
  - COMPLETE (unchanged)
  - Session/thread model (unchanged)
  - Supervisor routing (unchanged)

     sequential one-shot, IR renames delegates→invocations. → Revise fan-out approach

         This means they may have additional information, context or questions for you.
         Take their response into account and then reformulate the questions if appropriate.
         Start by asking them what they would like to clarify.

         Questions asked:
     - "What's your concern with the fan-out approach? The current proposal is: agent tasks become sequential one-shot (using INVOKE
     internally). What would you prefer?"
       (No answer provided)

⏺ What would you like to clarify or discuss about the fan-out approach?

  ⎿  Stop hook error: Failed with non-blocking status code:    
  /Users/prasannaarikala/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/ralph-loop/205b6e0b3036/hooks/stop-hook.sh: line 32:

✻ Baked for 7m 28s 
                                                                                                                                      
❯ shoudl have both options like child getting the appropriate sub task>> execute in parallel>>supervisor merging && child1 gets     
  input 1>> get child 1 response>>child 2 gets input 1 and response 1>> reponse2 >> supervisor merging?                               
                                                       
⏺ Ah, you're describing two distinct fan-out strategies: 
                                                               
  Strategy 1: Parallel (current behavior)              
  Supervisor sends subtasks to all children simultaneously                                                                            
    Child A gets subtask A → executes → result A                                                                                     
    Child B gets subtask B → executes → result B    (all concurrent)                                                                  
    Child C gets subtask C → executes → result C                                                                                      
  Supervisor merges all results                                                                                                       
                                                                                                                                      
  Strategy 2: Sequential/Chained (new)                                             
  Supervisor sends subtask to Child A
    Child A gets input → executes → result A
    Child B gets input + result A → executes → result B
    Child C gets input + result A + result B → executes → result C
  Supervisor merges final results

  The second is a pipeline — each child builds on the previous child's output. This is powerful for workflows like:
  - Research Agent → Analysis Agent → Report Agent
  - Data Extraction → Validation → Enrichment
  - Draft → Review → Finalize

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:      
  ⎿  · So fan-out should support both strategies, controlled by a parameter in the DSL. How should the strategy be expressed? → 
     Two separate constructs                           
                                                         
⏺ Clean separation. So the full agent-to-agent coordination vocabulary becomes:                                                       
                                                               
  ┌───────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┐     
  │ Construct │                        Semantics                        │ User talks to child? │           Execution            │
  ├───────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤     
  │ HANDOFF   │ Transfer the user to another agent                      │ Yes (interactive)    │ Sequential, child takes over   │
  ├───────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤     
  │ INVOKE    │ Ask an agent for a result                               │ No (one-shot)        │ Sequential, parent blocks      │     
  ├───────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤     
  │ FAN_OUT   │ Dispatch subtasks to multiple agents in parallel        │ No (one-shot each)   │ Parallel, parent merges        │     
  ├───────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤     
  │ PIPELINE  │ Chain agents sequentially, each builds on prior results │ No (one-shot each)   │ Sequential, results accumulate │     
  └───────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────┘     
                                                                                                                                      
  Four constructs, four clear mental models. No ambiguity about what each does.                                                       
                                                                                                                                      
  PIPELINE DSL syntax could look like:                                                                                                
                                                                                                                                      
  PIPELINE:
    - AGENT: Research_Agent
      INPUT: { topic: user_query }
      USE_RESULT: research_data

    - AGENT: Analysis_Agent
      INPUT: { data: research_data, criteria: analysis_criteria }
      USE_RESULT: analysis_result

    - AGENT: Report_Agent
      INPUT: { analysis: analysis_result, format: "executive_summary" }
      USE_RESULT: final_report

  Each stage's INPUT can reference previous stages' USE_RESULT variables. The runtime executes them in order, passing accumulated
  context.

  4 tasks (3 done, 1 in progress, 0 open)

  ✔ Ask clarifying questions about unification goals   
  ✔ Propose 2-3 unification approaches with trade-offs

 ☐ Final model

  (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Supervisor Orchestration in ABL

  The Kore.ai docs describe a central orchestrator that analyzes input, decomposes tasks, delegates to specialized agents, and
  aggregates results. ABL achieves this natively with the supervisor agent type.

  ---
  ABL Supervisor — Complete Example

  Here's an e-commerce customer service supervisor with three specialist agents:

  1. Supervisor Agent (customer_service_supervisor.abl)

  SUPERVISOR: CustomerServiceSupervisor

  GOAL: "Route customer requests to the right specialist. Greet the customer, understand their need, and delegate."
  PERSONA: "Friendly customer service coordinator"

  HANDOFF:
    - TO: BillingAgent
      WHEN: intent contains "bill" OR intent contains "payment" OR intent contains "refund"
      CONTEXT:
        pass: [customer_id, order_id]
        summary: "Customer has a billing/payment issue"
      RETURN: true

    - TO: TechnicalAgent
      WHEN: intent contains "error" OR intent contains "not working" OR intent contains "bug"
      CONTEXT:
        pass: [customer_id, product_id]
        summary: "Customer needs technical help"
      RETURN: true

    - TO: SalesAgent
      WHEN: intent contains "buy" OR intent contains "pricing" OR intent contains "upgrade"
      CONTEXT:
        pass: [customer_id]
        summary: "Customer interested in purchasing"
      RETURN: false

  COMPLETE:
    - WHEN: handoff.completed == true
      RESPOND: "Is there anything else I can help you with?"

  2. Specialist: Billing Agent (billing_agent.abl) — scripted flow

  AGENT: BillingAgent
  MODEL: claude-3-5-sonnet

  GOAL: "Resolve billing disputes and process refunds"
  PERSONA: "Empathetic billing specialist"

  FLOW:
    identify_issue:
      COLLECT:
        - issue_type: ENUM("overcharge", "refund", "payment_failed", "invoice_question")
          PROMPT: "What billing issue are you experiencing?"
      TRANSITION:
        - WHEN: issue_type == "refund" THEN process_refund
        - DEFAULT: lookup_account

    lookup_account:
      CALL: lookup_billing_account
        WITH: { customer_id: "{{customer_id}}" }
        STORE: account_details
      TRANSITION:
        - DEFAULT: resolve

    process_refund:
      CALL: initiate_refund
        WITH: { order_id: "{{order_id}}", customer_id: "{{customer_id}}" }
        STORE: refund_result
      RESPOND: "Your refund of {{refund_result.amount}} has been initiated. Reference: {{refund_result.id}}"
      TRANSITION:
        - DEFAULT: END

    resolve:
      RESPOND: "Here's what I found: {{account_details.summary}}. Your issue has been resolved."
      TRANSITION:
        - DEFAULT: END

  3. Specialist: Technical Agent (technical_agent.abl) — reasoning agent with tools

  AGENT: TechnicalAgent
  MODEL: claude-3-5-sonnet

  GOAL: "Diagnose and resolve technical issues"
  PERSONA: "Patient technical support engineer"

  TOOLS:
    - NAME: search_knowledge_base
      DESCRIPTION: "Search internal KB for known issues and solutions"
      PARAMETERS:
        query: { type: string, required: true }

    - NAME: check_system_status
      DESCRIPTION: "Check service health and recent incidents"
      PARAMETERS:
        service_name: { type: string, required: true }

    - NAME: create_ticket
      DESCRIPTION: "Escalate to engineering with a support ticket"
      PARAMETERS:
        title: { type: string, required: true }
        description: { type: string, required: true }
        priority: { type: string, default: "medium" }

  CONSTRAINTS:
    - NAME: always_search_first
      DESCRIPTION: "Always search the knowledge base before creating a ticket"
      ENFORCEMENT: strict

  ---
  How It Maps to the Kore.ai Pattern

  ┌──────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │      Kore.ai Docs Step       │                            ABL Equivalent                             │
  ├──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Input Reception              │ User message hits the supervisor agent's session                      │
  ├──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Orchestrator Analysis        │ Supervisor LLM evaluates HANDOFF WHEN conditions                      │
  ├──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Task Decomposition           │ CONTEXT: pass: [fields] selects what to send to child                 │
  ├──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Agent Selection & Delegation │ HANDOFF: TO: AgentName — routed by rules, LLM, or hybrid              │
  ├──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Parallel Execution           │ Child agent runs in its own execution context (scripted or reasoning) │
  ├──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Result Aggregation           │ RETURN: true sends results back to supervisor                         │
  ├──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Response Generation          │ COMPLETE: RESPOND: synthesizes the final answer                       │
  └──────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ---
  Routing Strategies

  ABL supports three strategies in the YAML config form:

  # 1. LLM-driven (default) — LLM reads agent descriptions and decides
  routing:
    strategy: llm

  # 2. Rules-based — regex pattern matching, no LLM call for routing
  routing:
    strategy: rules
    rules:
      - pattern: "bill|payment|refund"
        delegate_to: billing_agent

  # 3. Hybrid — rules first, LLM fallback for unmatched patterns
  routing:
    strategy: hybrid
    rules:
      - pattern: "bill|payment"
        delegate_to: billing_agent
    fallback: llm

  In ABL DSL syntax, the HANDOFF: WHEN: conditions serve as the routing rules, and the supervisor's LLM acts as the fallback when no
  condition matches.

  ---
  Hierarchical Supervisors (Multi-Level)

  Supervisors can delegate to other supervisors for complex domains:

  SUPERVISOR: TravelSupervisor

  HANDOFF:
    - TO: HotelSupervisor          # ← another supervisor
      WHEN: intent contains "hotel"
      RETURN: true

    - TO: FlightAgent              # ← leaf agent
      WHEN: intent contains "flight"
      RETURN: true

  SUPERVISOR: HotelSupervisor

  HANDOFF:
    - TO: HotelSearchAgent
      WHEN: intent contains "search" OR intent contains "find"
      RETURN: true

    - TO: HotelBookingAgent
      WHEN: intent contains "book" OR intent contains "reserve"
      RETURN: true

  This creates a routing tree: TravelSupervisor → HotelSupervisor → HotelSearchAgent.

  ---
  Escalation Handling

  ESCALATION:
    - CONDITION: agent_stuck          # child can't progress
      ACTION: retry_with_context      # retry with more context

    - CONDITION: user_frustrated      # sentiment detection
      ACTION: human_handoff           # transfer to live agent

    - CONDITION: max_turns_exceeded   # too many back-and-forth
      ACTION: summarize_and_handoff   # summarize and escalate

  ---
  Key Differences from Kore.ai UI Config

  1. Code-first: ABL is a declarative DSL (.abl files), not a drag-and-drop UI — it's version-controlled and composable
  2. Compile step: ABL compiles to an Intermediate Representation (IR) that the runtime executes — supervisors become regular agents
  with a routing config
  3. Mode preservation: Child agents keep their execution mode (scripted agents stay scripted, reasoning stays reasoning) across
  handoff boundaries
  4. RETURN semantics: Explicit control over whether a handoff is temporary (RETURN: true — control comes back) or permanent (RETURN:
   false — one-way transfer)

⏺ Ran 1 stop hook (ctrl+o to expand)
  ⎿  Stop hook error: Failed with non-blocking status code:
  /Users/prasannaarikala/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/ralph-loop/205b6e0b3036/hooks/stop-hook.sh: line 32:
  jq: command not found

✻ Cogitated for 2m 33s

❯ review the code to confirm if this decomposition and merging results is really happening for every input --  

⏺ Explore(Trace supervisor execution path)
  ⎿  Done (44 tool uses · 72.4k tokens · 2m 9s)
  (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Verdict: The Kore.ai Docs Pattern is NOT Fully Implemented

  Here's what the runtime actually does vs what the documentation claims:

  What ACTUALLY Happens Per Input

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │            Kore.ai Claim            │                                      ABL Reality                                       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Task Decomposition — breaks into    │ Not automatic. Default: routes to ONE agent per turn. Multi-agent fan-out only         │
  │ subtasks                            │ triggers if the LLM happens to emit multiple __handoff__ tool calls in one turn.       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Parallel Execution — agents run     │ Conditional. Fan-out exists (__fan_out__ in routing-executor.ts) but it's not the      │
  │ independently                       │ default path. The LLM must explicitly request multiple handoffs.                       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Result Aggregation — orchestrator   │ LLM-driven, not programmatic. Fan-out results are formatted as text and sent back to   │
  │ collects, resolves conflicts        │ the LLM. There's no automated merge or conflict resolution logic.                      │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Response Generation — unified       │ The LLM synthesizes it, not the runtime. Results are stored in _last_fan_out /         │
  │ response synthesized                │ _fan_out_result_{agent} but the LLM decides what to do with them.                      │
  └─────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  The Actual Execution Flow

  User: "I need a refund and my app is crashing"
                      ↓
           Supervisor LLM Turn
                      ↓
       ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
       │  DEFAULT (most likely):     │
       │  LLM picks ONE agent       │
       │  __handoff__ → BillingAgent │
       │  (ignores the crash issue)  │
       └─────────────────────────────┘

       ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
       │  BEST CASE (if LLM emits   │
       │  two __handoff__ calls):    │
       │  reasoning-executor.ts:491  │
       │  synthesizes → __fan_out__  │
       │  → BillingAgent + TechAgent │
       │  → results aggregated       │
       │  → LLM synthesizes response │
       └─────────────────────────────┘

  The Gap

  The 7-step orchestration pattern (input → analysis → decomposition → selection → parallel execution → aggregation → response) from
  the Kore.ai docs is aspirational, not fully realized:

  1. Decomposition is LLM-discretionary — there's no programmatic step that analyzes a message and splits it into subtasks. The LLM
  must decide on its own to call multiple handoffs.
  2. No explicit multi-intent detection — routing-executor.ts has no intent classification step before routing. The
  intent_classification config in the IR schema is compiled but the runtime relies entirely on the LLM's judgment.
  3. Aggregation is passive — results are stored in session data (_last_fan_out) and formatted as text for the LLM, but there's no
  programmatic conflict resolution, deduplication, or coherence check.
  4. disableParallelToolUse: true is set for supervisors (reasoning-executor.ts:311), which actually discourages the LLM from
  emitting multiple handoffs in one turn — working against the parallel decomposition pattern.

  What Would Be Needed for True Decomposition + Aggregation

  To match the Kore.ai docs pattern, the runtime would need:

  1. Explicit intent decomposition step — before routing, classify the message into N intents and create N subtasks programmatically
  2. Automatic fan-out — when N > 1 intents detected, always fan-out (don't rely on LLM tool calls)
  3. Programmatic result aggregation — merge child results with conflict detection, not just "let the LLM figure it out"
  4. Remove disableParallelToolUse for supervisors, or add a dedicated multi-intent routing strategy

  The infrastructure for parallel fan-out exists and works — the gap is in automatically triggering it rather than leaving it to LLM
  discretion.

  ⎿  Stop hook error: Failed with non-blocking status code:
  /Users/prasannaarikala/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/ralph-loop/205b6e0b3036/hooks/stop-hook.sh: line 32:
  jq: command not found

✻ Churned for 2m 23s

❯ what is teh current support for multi-intent detection. we had the support 

⏺ Explore(Find multi-intent detection code)
  ⎿  Done (24 tool uses · 95.5k tokens · 1m 6s)
  (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Multi-intent detection is fully implemented. Here's the current support:

  What Exists

  5 Strategies

  ┌────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │        Strategy        │                     Behavior                     │                    Agent Type                    │
  ├────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ primary_queue          │ Handle top intent, queue rest with TTL (10 min)  │ All                                              │
  │ (default)              │                                                  │                                                  │
  ├────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ sequential             │ Process intents one-by-one with context          │ All                                              │
  │                        │ flow-through                                     │                                                  │
  ├────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ parallel               │ Fan-out to multiple agents simultaneously        │ Supervisor only (downgrades to sequential for    │
  │                        │                                                  │ others)                                          │
  ├────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ disambiguate           │ Ask user to choose between detected intents      │ All                                              │
  ├────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ auto                   │ LLM assesses intent relationships → picks        │ All                                              │
  │                        │ strategy                                         │                                                  │
  └────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Auto Strategy Resolution

  The LLM classifies intent relationships, then multi-intent-strategy.ts resolves:

  independent + supervisor → parallel (fan-out)
  independent + non-supervisor → sequential
  dependent (any agent) → sequential
  ambiguous (any agent) → disambiguate

  Detection → Dispatch Flow

  User message
    ↓
  NLU: parseIntentResponse() → { primary, alternatives[], relationships }
    ↓
  dispatchMultiIntentIfNeeded() [flow-step-executor.ts]
    ↓
  handleMultiIntent() [routing-executor.ts]
    ↓
    ├─ primary_queue → route primary + enqueueIntents(alternatives)
    ├─ sequential → build executionPlan[] + queue all
    ├─ parallel (supervisor) → fanOutTasks[] → handleFanOut()
    ├─ parallel (non-supervisor) → downgrade to sequential
    └─ disambiguate → present choices to user

  Config Hierarchy

  Agent-level (DSL: MULTI_INTENT section)
    ↓ overrides
  Project-level (DB: ProjectRuntimeConfig)
    ↓ overrides
  Platform defaults: { enabled: true, strategy: 'primary_queue', max_intents: 3, confidence_threshold: 0.6, queue_max_age_ms: 600_000
   }

  Intent Queue (intent-queue.ts)

  - Duplicates merged (higher confidence wins)
  - Sorted by confidence descending
  - TTL-based expiry (pruneExpired)
  - dequeueNext() pops highest-confidence intent for next turn

  ---
  So Back to the Original Question

  The infrastructure does exist to do decomposition + parallel execution for supervisors. The gap I identified earlier was about the
  default path — here's the nuance:

  What works today

  1. NLU detects multiple intents with confidence scores and relationships
  2. Strategy resolver picks the right approach based on agent type + relationship
  3. Supervisor + parallel strategy → handleFanOut() executes multiple agents concurrently
  4. Fan-out results aggregated in _last_fan_out and sent to LLM for synthesis

  The disconnect

  The issue is where multi-intent detection triggers. It lives in flow-step-executor.ts:321 (dispatchMultiIntentIfNeeded), which
  means:

  - Scripted agents with ON_INPUT branches — multi-intent fires during input processing
  - Reasoning/supervisor agents without a flow — the trigger path is through the NLU intent classification in the routing layer, but
  the disableParallelToolUse: true flag on supervisors (reasoning-executor.ts:311) works against the LLM naturally emitting multiple
  handoffs

  So the system has two parallel mechanisms:
  1. NLU-driven multi-intent (programmatic, strategy-based) — fully built
  2. LLM-driven multiple __handoff__ calls (ad-hoc) — suppressed by disableParallelToolUse

  The NLU path is the correct one and it's complete. The earlier analysis was focused on the LLM tool-call path, which is
  intentionally constrained because the NLU path is the proper entry point for multi-intent.

  jq: command not found

✻ Sautéed for 1m 39s

❯ assume disableParallelToolUse is set to false, when parallel handoff happen does the next prompt from child agent go to        
  customer or supervisor? The pattern in the documentation refers to response being always merged by supervisor. do we do that?  

⏺ Searched for 2 patterns, read 2 files (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Now let me see the critical part — what happens to child agent responses during fan-out execution. Specifically, does
  executeMessage inside a fan-out child talk directly to the customer?

⏺ Read 2 files (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Now let me check the critical question — does onChunk (the streaming callback to the customer) fire during fan-out child execution?

⏺ Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Now the key question — does handleFanOut pass onChunk down to child executeMessage calls?

⏺ Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Now let me check handleHandoff to see if child agents stream directly to customer there:

⏺ Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Now I have the full picture. Let me also check formatFanOutToolResult:

⏺ Searched for 1 pattern (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Here's the definitive answer:

  Handoff vs Fan-Out: Two Very Different Behaviors

  Single Handoff (__handoff__) — Child talks DIRECTLY to customer

  routing-executor.ts:547-551

  const result = await this.ctx.executeMessage(
    session.id,
    messageToForward,
    onChunk,        // ← customer's streaming callback passed through!
    onTraceEvent,
  );

  onChunk is passed directly to the child agent. The child agent's response streams straight to the customer. The supervisor does NOT
   see or merge the response — the child's output IS the final output.

  After the child completes, the supervisor only gets a result.response string, and if it's non-empty, breakLoop = true (line 824) —
  the supervisor's reasoning loop exits without synthesizing anything.

  The only exception: if the child returns an empty response, the supervisor continues its loop and can respond itself.

  Fan-Out (__fan_out__) — Supervisor merges, but with a critical caveat

  routing-executor.ts:1616-1620

  const executePromise = this.ctx.executeMessage(
    childSessionId,
    unit.message,
    undefined,       // ← onChunk is NOT passed! Children can't stream to customer
    fanOutTraceEvent,
  );

  Fan-out children get undefined for onChunk. Their responses are captured silently, aggregated, and returned to the supervisor's LLM
   as a tool result:

  routing-executor.ts:854-855

  // Format as structured text so the LLM can synthesize a unified response
  toolResult = this.routing.formatFanOutToolResult(result);

  routing-executor.ts:863

  // Do NOT set breakLoop — results go back to LLM for synthesis

  The formatted result looks like:
  Fan-out completed: 2/2 tasks succeeded.

  [BillingAgent] SUCCESS: Your refund of $50 has been initiated.
  [TechnicalAgent] SUCCESS: The crash is caused by a known bug, fix deployed.

  Synthesize a single unified response covering all results above.

  The supervisor's LLM then generates a merged response.

  The Problem

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────┐
  │                       Path                       │               Who talks to customer?               │  Supervisor merges?  │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
  │ Single __handoff__                               │ Child agent directly                               │ No — supervisor      │
  │                                                  │                                                    │ exits                │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
  │ Fan-out (__fan_out__)                            │ Supervisor synthesizes                             │ Yes — LLM-driven     │
  │                                                  │                                                    │ merge                │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
  │ disableParallelToolUse: false + LLM emits 2      │ Converted to fan-out (line 491) → Supervisor       │ Yes                  │
  │ handoffs                                         │ synthesizes                                        │                      │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────┘

  So the Kore.ai pattern (supervisor always merges) only works for the fan-out path. The default single-handoff path completely
  bypasses the supervisor — the child agent takes over the conversation and streams directly to the customer.

  This means for the common case (single intent → single handoff), the supervisor is not aggregating or synthesizing anything. It's a
   full context switch to the child agent. The supervisor pattern documented by Kore.ai only holds when multi-intent triggers
  fan-out.

  jq: command not found

## My request for Codex:
I need to add benefits to the customer about the new agent platform. This deck is not good enough. The overview file has some context. additionally we can add these Benefits are not clear. We need to emphasize -- rearchitected for lower hardware footprint (only for VPC customers), faster development cycles for agents, easy to troubleshoot and fix, more comprehensive version management and deployment management, build, deploy and manage agents with any IDE or AI (Arch works with any frontier model), enhanced comprehensive analytics (with NLU driven dashboards), enhanced observability. I need a more comprehensive story for existing customers.
